REASON FOR EEG:  Rule out seizures.

TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The EEG opens to a diffuse slow background with an asymmetric pattern over each hemisphere.  The left hemisphere is mainly in the theta-delta frequencies with maximum slowing seen in the left midtemporal region.  The right hemisphere reveals a low-voltage discharge with minimal normal cortical frequencies seen, mainly in the delta frequency.
No normal posterior dominant rhythm is seen and normal frontocentral beta is absent.  At times the patient coughs and the frequencies appeared to improve to a theta pattern.  No normal sleep architecture was seen, and no activating procedures were performed.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Left hemispheric slowing maximum on the midtemporal region.
Right hemispheric attenuation.
Infrequent sharply contoured C3 sharp transients, possibly asymmetric vertex.
SEIZURES  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Left temporal slowing.
Right hemispheric attenuation.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of focal structural dysfunction in the left hemisphere, maximum in the temporal region, which is nonspecific with regard to etiology but has a high correlation with a lesion in that region.  The right-sided attenuation may be due to cortical dysfunction sometimes seen in stroke or overlying structural lesion like a subdural hemorrhage causing decreased attenuation.  Clinical correlation was recommended.  No seizures were seen.

subject: aaaaandb
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  69 year old right handed woman admitted on the 27th after she hit her head on the refrigerator, was found to have a facial contusion and then an aortic arch dissection.  CT scan with previous evidence of atherosclerosis.
MEDICATIONS:  Midazolam, Fentanyl, Isoflurane, Vecuronium, Lidocaine, Solu-Medrol, Magnesium.
INTRODUCTION:  Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside in the OR using standard CEA montage with 1 channel EKG.  The recording begins at 8:35 and concludes at 12:35.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  As the tracing opens, the patient has been intubated and sedated.  The background EEG demonstrates a mixed frequency pattern with beta, theta, and delta.  Cooling begins at 9:38.  With a combination of cooling and anesthetic medications, the EEG transitions to a burst suppression pattern at 9:16 a.m.  The bursts of cerebral activity are sometimes asynchronous.  They may be longer on the left than the right.  There are prominent shifting asymmetries and some sharp activities in the left temporal region.
The surgeon is informed of the burst suppression pattern.
Head temperature 27 degrees at 9:56.  This is associated with the burst suppression pattern.
At 10:15, the EEG becomes suppressed and the surgeon is informed when the patient has been suppressed for 5 minutes.  Circulatory arrest is initiated at that time.
Passive rewarming begins at 10:45.  With passive rewarming, there are some EEG electrode challenges including loss of electrode integrity (the patient's head was packed in ice to achieve the sufficiently cooled cranial temperature).  There are some shifting asymmetries and then in the left section of the record, the electrode integrity is compromised to the point that limited conclusions should be made regarding asymmetry.
Recording concludes at 12:31 after the cross clamp has been removed at 11:52 and reperfusion is underway.  The heart is shocked at 11:54 which produces artifact.
IMPRESSION/CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This 4-hour EEG monitoring during aortic arch repair and hypothermic treatment was remarkable for sustained period of electrocerebral silence.
The shifting asymmetries noted should be interpreted with caution given some of the electrode problems, but they may be associated with this patient's underlying cerebrovascular disease.

subject: aaaaankd
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  42 year old right handed male with history of aortic aneurysm, hypertension, asthma, going to the OR for aortic root replacement.

MEDICATIONS:  Midazolam, Fentanyl, Propofol, Isoflurane, Ceftriaxone, Vancomycin, Solu Medrol, Lidocaine, Potassium, Norepinephrine

REASON FOR STUDY:  Aortic arch bypass surgery.

INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrodes during aortic bypass surgery.  The patient was recorded during the entire procedure.  The technician was present during the entire procedure and the physician was present during the critical parts of the procedure.  Feedback was given to the surgeon about changes in the EEG.

TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  During the procedure the right side of the head is cooled more than the left which may effect the reading of the EEG as was noted during this case.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a baseline posterior dominant rhythm that reaches 9 Hz which is reactive to eye opening.  Normal frontal central beta seen.  As the procedure goes on, the incision occurs at 9:16 in the morning, heparin is given at 9:31, bypass of the head and body is done at 9:51, aorta clamped at 9:54, cooling starts at 9:56.  During all this time the EEG is at baseline, however, around 9:56 when the cooling begins, there started to be noted some slowing, specifically around 10:01 a.m. in the morning there is an asymmetry where the right side is slightly slower than the left with some sharply contoured activity in the temporal region, that at times becomes periodic and is not clearly seen with a lower amplitude.  This pattern continues as the left hemisphere also starts to become slow, but remains asymmetric up to the time where the EEG starts becomes completely suppressed which occurs first on the right hemisphere, but completely on the entire brain at around 10:13.  At this time there is some burst suppression and by 10:25 it is in complete suppression.  There is cardiac arrest at 10:30 a.m. and he starts to be rewarmed at 10:47 a.m.  He is removed from head bypass at 10:08 a.m. and noticeable bursts of activity start to be seen at around 11:04.  The EEG returns to baseline by 11:21:36 and there is no longer an asymmetry over the right hemisphere.

All the changes that were discussed above were discussed and described to the surgeon.

IMPRESSION:  This is an EEG recording during aortic root replacement surgery with a mild asymmetry noted while cooling, thought to be related to cooling artifact of the right side of the head predominating compared to the left, which after surgery and burst suppression was achieved appeared to return to baseline.  A normal EEG at baseline was seen at the end of the surgery.

CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This is an uneventful aortic root replacement surgery EEG recording.

subject: aaaaaods
Clinical Report:
TYPE OF STUDY:  Routine EEG.
DIAGNOSES:  Memory problems, forgetfulness, and agitation.
HISTORY:  This is a 59-year-old right-handed woman with past medical history significant for dementia and asthma, who presents for worsening of forgetfulness and agitation.
INTRODUCTION:  A digital EEG was performed in the lab using the standard international 10-20 system of electrode placement in addition to 1 channel of EKG monitoring.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.  These tracing captures wakefulness through short period of drowsiness.  In the most alert state, the alpha rhythm is 9 to 9.5 hertz in frequency, 30-40 microvolts in amplitude, seen in the occipital region, which attenuates with eye opening.  There is a small amount of low amplitude frontocentral beta activity, which is distributed symmetrically.  Drowsiness is characterized by disappearance of the alpha rhythm, slowing of the background, and absence of muscle and eye-blink artifacts.  There are intermittent periods of bitemporal slowing at F7/T3 and F8/T4 during wakefulness and short period of drowsiness consisting of 3 hertz delta frequency, 50-60 microvolt in amplitude lasting 1-2 seconds.  Hyperventilation resulted in slowing seen in most cerebral hemispheres.  Photic stimulation was performed at multiple fresh frequencies and resulting in a driving response.  Heart rate was 70-80 beats per minute and regular.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to bitemporal slowing during wakefulness and short period of drowsiness.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  The above findings are suggestive of bilateral structural abnormalities or bilateral cerebral dysfunction.  Other causes included toxic metabolic or infectious encephalopathy, or degenerative causes.  There were no epileptiform abnormalities or seizures noted during this study.  The abscess of epileptiform abnormalities does not exclude to possibility of epilepsy or intermittent seizures.  If seizure remains a clinical concern, a repeat EEG capturing either stages of sleep or a 24-hour ambulatory EEG monitoring may be helpful.

The patient has intermittent arousals with the suggestion of a 7-8 Hz alpha rhythm.  Shifting asymmetries were noted.  In addition, intermittent attenuations were noted. Background slowing and disorganization.
Shifting slowing.
Intermittent attenuations.

subject: aaaaaore
Sleep patterns: vertex waves, K complex,  POSTs, spindles
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 34-year-old woman with impaired cognitive thinking processes and progressive left-sided numbness.
MEDICATIONS:  Dyazide.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG is performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed.  The technologist noted that this patient seemed to have stable cognition throughout the EEG.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In wakefulness, there is a low voltage 10 Hz alpha rhythm in the background with generous low voltage beta.  The patient seemed to be a bit drowsy, transitioning into stage I sleep with an increase in beta and in vertex waves followed by arousal.  Hyperventilation did not significantly active the record.  In fact, the patient seemed to be falling asleep at the end of hyperventilation with beta spindles characteristic of stage II sleep.  Other features of sleep included vertex waves and more subtle POSTS.  Sustained stage II sleep was more abundant in those vertex waves, K complexes, and POSTS were noted.
The patient remained drowsy throughout photic stimulation which nonetheless, elicited a driving response.  By the end of photic stimulation, she was drifting back into stage II sleep.
Heart rate 66 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal sleep EEG.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No epileptiform features were identified.  This patient appears to be extremely sleepy.  Clinical correlation for hypersomnolence may be appropriate, but a sleep-deprived EEG could also produce these findings.

subject: aaaaaott
Clinical Report:

INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG is performed in the lab using 10-20 system of electrode placement with one-channel EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In the most alert state, the posterior dominant rhythm was 8 to 8.5 Hz in frequency seen in the occipital region, which attenuates with eye opening.  There is 6-7 Hz diffuse theta activity seen during wakefulness, which is distributed symmetrically.  There is a small amount of low-amplitude frontal central beta activity, which is distributed symmetrically.  Drowsiness is characterized by disappearance of the posterior dominant rhythm, slowing of the background, and absence of muscle and eye blink artifact.  There are intermittent bursts of bitemporal slowing at F7/T3 and F8/T4 more prominent on the left side or independent left temporal slowing at F7/T3 during wakefulness and drowsiness.  Heart rate was 80 beats per minute and regular.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Diffuse slowing of the background with theta activity seen in wakefulness and drowsiness, and slowing of the posterior dominant rhythm.
Bitemporal slowing, left more than right side seen during wakefulness and drowsiness.  No epileptiform abnormalities have been identified in this record.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  The above findings could be due to toxic, metabolic, hypoxic, or infectious encephalopathy.  Alternatively, it could be due to effect of sedating medication or bilateral cerebral dysfunction or degenerative brain disease. Bilateral temporal slowing that could be due to bilateral structural abnormalities or bilateral cerebral dysfunction.  No epileptiform abnormalities or seizures are identified in this record.  This does not rule out the diagnosis of epilepsy or intermittent seizures.  If clinically indicated, a repeat EEG capturing deeper stages of sleep or continuous bedside EEG monitoring may be helpful.

subject: aaaaaovh

MEDICATIONS:  Protonix, Captopril, morphine, others.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG is performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  This is a awake and drowsy record in a confused individual.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not possible.

Features of drowsiness include an increase in theta.  As the patient transitions in and out of stage I sleep, more sleep, irregular delta is noted and the vertex waves that were recorded are somewhat broader than usual.  As the patient transitions to deeper Stage I sleep, more conventional-appearing symmetric vertex waves were noted.

Abnormal electroencephalogram due to #1, background slowing and disorganization.
Slowing the alpha rhythm in apparent wakefulness.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No epileptiform features were identified in this record. The background slowing and disorganization is a nonspecific finding in a subject of this age and can be due to toxic or metabolic encephalopathy or other, modest diffuse bihemispheric brain process. There were no specific hallmarks of a metabolic encephalopathy.

subject: aaaaaoxn
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is an 81-year-old male with fever and change of mental status.  No other clinical information available.
MEDICATIONS:  Cardizem, Reglan, vancomycin.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside in the ICU using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient was relatively somnolent.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The background EEG demonstrates a slow, mixed frequency pattern with theta and delta.  There are frontally predominant triphasic waves.  There are also some slow periodic complexes that appear in the right posterior quadrant more so than the left.  Stimulation of the patient produces a clear alerting response with a bit more fast theta frequency activity and a suggestion of a 6 Hz posterior dominant rhythm.  When the patient drifts off to sleep, the EEG becomes more significantly slow with more rhythmic slow mixed theta delta.
Heart rate irregular 72 beats per minute.
IMPRESSION:  This is a markedly abnormal EEG for an adult of this age due to:
Marked background slowing.
Frontally predominant triphasic waves.
Occasional posteriorly predominant periodic complexes seen primarily on the right.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  Overall, this EEG is most suggestive of a toxic or metabolic pattern.  The triphasic waves improve with tactile stimulation and the patient shows a clear alerting with stimulation.  If seizures are an important consideration, additional EEG recording may be of value if this patient is thought to have seizures, particularly as  there is a right posterior periodic complex noted intermittently.

subject: aaaaapre
Clinical Report:
Total length of the recording: about 16 hours.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 53-year-old man status post cardiac resuscitation on 08/06/2013, who is now undergoing hypothermia protocol.  His current body temperature is 93.8 degrees.
MEDICATIONS:  Include propofol drip and Ativan and others.
INTRODUCTION:  The recording is performed according to the 10-20 system with additional T1 and T2 electrodes and a single EKG lead.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not performed.  The recording was reviewed at least once during the recording by the attending physician.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORDING:  There is no posterior dominant rhythm and no anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient.  Early in the recording, diffuse EMG artifact is present that obscures multiple leads and limits the interpretation of the recording during those times.  The degree of EMG artifact decreases as the recording progresses, and stops altogether at approximately 9 p.m.  It is unclear why EMG artifact is disappears since no notation is made on the EEG recording, however, it is possible that the patient might have received a paralytic agent.  The background consists of bursts of polymorphic theta and delta activity that are low in amplitude admixed with a period of background discontinuity.  As the recording progresses, bursts of activity shorten and periods of discontinuity increase and the patient finally reaches the burst to suppression ratio.  As the recording progresses, the burst to suppression ratio interval increases gradually, and bursts become shorter and less well formed.  At approximately 6:25 a.m., rhythmic diffuse activity is noted on the EEG.  Based on the video view, it appears to correspond to possible CPR administration.  It discontinues until about 6:47 a.m.  Once this CPR artifact stops, there is no discernible cerebral activity and there is also no heart rate that is picked on the single EKG lead.  Of note, prior to CPR, a single EKG lead demonstrated a heart rate of approximately 30 beats per minute.
FINDINGS:  Diffuse background slowing that increases gradually as the recording progresses.  No clear discernible cerebral activity after approximately 6:47 a.m.
IMPRESSION:  This is a very abnormal 16 hour continuous video EEG monitoring due to worsening of background slowing and burst suppression that finally progressed to complete suppression of all background activity.  Sinus bradycardia is noted on a single EKG lead.  After 6:47 a.m., the EKG lead does not demonstrate any heartbeat.

subject: aaaaabdn
Clinical Report:
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 54-year-old woman with refractory seizures since age 30, morbid obesity, and obstructive sleep apnea.
MEDICATIONS:  Aspirin, lovastatin, carbamazepine, Dilantin, omeprazole, vitamins.
INTRODUCTION:  A routine EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded while awake and asleep. Activating procedures included hyperventilation and photic stimulation.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The records opens to a posterior dominant rhythm that at best reaches 9 Hz which is reactive to eye opening.  There is some frontal central beta.  There is limited wakefulness of the recording.  The majority of the time the patient is drowsy and sometimes even during wakefulness there appears to be some excessive theta seen throughout.  The patient is recorded in stage I sleep.
Activating procedures, including hyperventilation, exacerbates left temporal slowing, which I will discuss, with abnormal discharges.  Photic stimulation produces no abnormal discharges.
Abnormal discharges:
Focal sharp waves seen over the left hemisphere maximal at T3 and at times at T5, sometimes more anteriorly at F7.  All of them are with prominent posterior parietal P3 component and high amplitude.  These are seen in wakefulness and sleep.
Focal slowing seen over the left hemisphere at T3, sometimes more posteriorly at T5, at a frequency of 2-4 Hz and amplitude of 20-60 mV.
Excessive daytime wakefulness is seen at a frequency of 5-7 Hz at an amplitude of 20-60 mV.
Heart rate:  The heart is regular at a frequency of 72.
Seizures:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to left hemispheric sharp waves, sometimes with a more posterior component, as well as left mid temporal slowing and excessive daytime wakefulness.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with the diagnosis of localization related epilepsy with seizures likely arising from the left hemisphere.  In addition, there is evidence of focal cerebral dysfunction in the left mid temporal to posterior temporal regions, which is nonspecific in regards to etiology.  Lastly, there is some evidence of diffuse cerebral dysfunction, which is nonspecific in regards to etiology.  No seizures were seen.
Note:  This EEG is slightly different from the one recorded on 02/24/2011 where there was some bilateral slowing and some sharply contoured left temporal slowing.

There is a small amount of shifting slowing, but there is also underlying focal slowing in the left anterior temporal region.  Hyperventilation accentuates the left anterior temporal focal slowing. IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Left anterior temporal focal slowing.
Shifting slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  Only a single, definitive sharp wave was identified.  This EEG supports a bihemispheric process, but the focal slowing may have lateralizing or localizing importance.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 70 year old right handed female with recurrent seizures.
MEDICATIONS: Carbamazepine, Phenobarbital, ASA
INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. This EEG was Initiated on the evening of the 10th following repetitive complex partial seizures.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: Day #1, September 10 to September 11, 2010: This initial section demonstrates, particularly in the early evening, repetitive complex partial seizures from the right temporal region with characteristic evolution of rhythmic theta frequency activity and sharp waves. In the steep recording, the seizures abate but the next morning they are seen intermittently, particularly at 9:00 a.m. They last 1 to 2 minutes. They seem to decrease as the patient drifts off to sleep. The patient is out of status epilepticus throughout much of the morning but between 9:00 and 10:00 a.m. she does experience relatively frequent complex partial seizures. Stimulation of the patient at 10:15 produces an arousal but no additional seizures.
Day #2, September 11 to September 12, 2010: This section of the record begins shortly after 10:00 am. Seizures typically lasting approximately 1 minute are identified but are not continuous. There are significant sections of the record without seizures. The patient received additional doses of carbamazepine and phenobarbital. Complex partial seizure out of sleep characterized by faster frequency activity in the beta frequency range is noted at 3:29a.m., and this seizure localizes a bit more to the patients structural abnormality in the posterior temporal and the cortical area. This section of the EEG is terminated at 14:45, when the patient goes to MRI scan, but the seizures have not abated and she continues to have complex partial seizures at 14:42. While there is waxing and waning of the overall seizure frequency, at that section of the record, her seizures are more frequent and, in fact, are frequent enough to meet the criteria for complex partial status epilepticus.
LONG TERM MONITORING 01/13/2010 to 09/16/2010
INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG. The patient received phenobarbital and carbamazepine.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: Sections of the record on the 13th through the early morning of the 14th show Intermittent complex partial seizures emanating from the right hemisphere, particularly mid to the posterior temporal region with evolution of faster alpha and then beta frequency activity. Most seizures are on the order of 1 to 2 minutes in duration.   Some, but not all, seizures are clinically significant and are associated with head turning to the left and confusion. At least in the ear1y section of the record, the patient has a mild left hemiparesis. She demonstrates persistent left HOMONYMOUS hemianopsia and transient left sided weakness. Intermittent focal slowing is noted from the right hemisphere and the overall background includes excess beta and a small amount of theta. The seizures are Intermittent, not continuous, but may be seen more than once per hour up until 11 :00 a.m., at which point they seem to have abated.
HR: 90bpm

No additional clinical seizures are noted.

1. Initially frequent seizures with abrupt reduction in seizures.

subject: aaaaabhz
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  70 year old right handed woman with history of complex partial seizures, stroke, diabetes who presents with seizures.
MEDICATIONS:  Tegretol, Diltiazem, Lorazepam, Lisinopril, Dyazide, Fexofenadine, ASA
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in lab using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded in wakefulness and drowsiness.
Activating procedures, including photic stimulation, were performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a well defined posterior dominant rhythm that reaches 9 Hz which is reactive to eye opening, but best seen over the left hemisphere with slight decrease amplitude over the right.  There is frontal central beta seen best over the left hemisphere also.  Overall there is attenuation of faster and normal frequencies of the entire right hemisphere, but maximally in the temporal and frontal regions.  The patient appears to go into drowsiness, stage I, sleep.
Activating procedures, including photic stimulation, produces no abnormal discharges.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal slow waves seen at F7/T3 frequently at a frequency of 2-4 Hz with an amplitude of 20-60 mV.  At times they become rhythmic and sharply contoured.
Right hemispheric attenuation and decrease of faster frequencies.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Right hemisphere attenuation and intermittent left anterior temporal slow waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of focal cerebral dysfunction in the left anterior temporal region, which is nonspecific with regards to etiology.  In addition, the attenuation seen over the right hemisphere is consistent with a known stroke and cortical damage in that region.  No seizures or epileptiform discharges were seen.

subject: aaaaabju
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: This is a 63-year-old woman with previous EEGs demonstrating right temporal sharp waves of delta and theta.  The patient reports that her last big seizure was five months ago, which was a nocturnal seizure with tongue biting and incontinence but daytime episodes of vague, staring with glassy eyes, and humming.  The patient is amnestic for these events.
MEDICATIONS: Dilantin, Neurontin, and Teneral (patient reports non-compliance).
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG is performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG. Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed.  This is an awake and drowsy record.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The background EEG demonstrates some marked intrahemispheric asymmetry.  The activity from the left hemisphere is well organized with a posterior dominant rhythm, which can reach up to 11 Hz.  From the right hemisphere, the posterior dominant rhythm is 9 Hz.  There is much more arrhythmic delta activity from the right hemisphere particularly in the mid-to-anterior temporal regions with high amplitude spike and slow wave discharges identified in wakefulness.  These may occur as isolated discharges or in brief trains. There are also bursts of rhythmic theta again identified in the right temporal region. The bursts of either rhythmic mixed theta/delta or sharp waves are under ten seconds in duration.  Hyperventilation is performed early on in the record and does appear to accentuate the epileptiform activity and the rhythmic slowing described above, as well as producing some slowing from the contralateral hemisphere.  Following hyperventilation, as the patient becomes drowsy, there is an increase in background theta.  Other features of drowsiness include rhythmic theta, as well as an increase in background beta.  Of note, in wakefulness, there are rare left anterior temporal sharp and slow wave complexes but the right-to-left ratio is on the order of perhaps 100 to 1.
Photic stimulation elicits a vigorous driving response even at 1 Hz.
HR:  42 BPM.
IMPRESSION: Abnormal EEG due to:
Prominent right temporal spike and slow activity as well as focal slowing and rhythmic right temporal theta.
Rare left temporal sharp waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: This tracing supports a partial mechanism for epilepsy with an underlying structural abnormality and a very active epileptogenic focus.  Given the frequency of epileptiform activity identified in this record, consideration for EEG monitoring would be appropriate.

subject: aaaaacrz
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  64 year old right handed male with a history of seizures, here for evaluation of seizure localization and frequency.
MEDICATIONS:  Phenobarbital, Tegretol.
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video long-term EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrodes.  The patient was recorded in wakefulness and sleep.  No activating procedures were performed.  Continuous spike wave detection as well as seizure detection software was used during the EEG.  In addition, patient alarms as well as nurses' notes were also used during the EEG.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a mildly diffuse slow background with a posterior dominant rhythm that reaches a frequency of 8 Hz, which appears to be reactive to eye opening.  There is some frontocentral beta.  The patient is recorded in wakefulness and sleep.  No activating procedures were performed.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal slow waves seen in the left mid-temporal region at T3 at a frequency of 2 to 4 Hz and an amplitude of 20 to 50 microvolts seen occasionally.
In addition, focal sharp waves are seen infrequently at T3, mainly in sleep.
EVENTS:  At 1:24 in the morning, the patient had an event.  In the video it appears that the patient suddenly becomes agitated and wants to go home.  He stands up abruptly from the bed and starts attacking the nurses and appears to want to stand up and leave.  He stands up out of the bed and disappears from the video.  Minutes later he comes back and he is restrained.  He remains agitated and moves around the bed during the rest of the recording.  During the time the patient becomes agitated and minutes prior becoming agitated, the EEG remains normal with no epileptiform discharges or seizures seen.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Left temporal slowing and sharp waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with a diagnosis of localization related epilepsy, with seizures likely arising from the left temporal region.  In addition, the episode captured agitation but did not reveal any epileptic discharges and is likely a nonepileptic event.  No seizures were captured during this day 1 of EEG monitoring.

The spiking seems to wane and then gradually builds up in the lateral temporal grid in contacts 11, 12, 13, 14 and then more gradually involving contacts in the mesial temporal grid, which continues to include 3, 4, 5, 16, 17, 18.  During this ictal discharge, the patient continues to speak and to put away the phone at 11:20:36.  As she does so, the epileptiform activity demonstrates a much more significant spread.  Primarily in the third strip, which begins with contacts 11, 12 and also in the lateral strip 16, 17, but has a more diffuse spread in the mesial temporal chain now involving contacts 1 through 5, 6 through 8, and 16 through 18.  The superior two electrodes are less involved.  The right temporal chain is relatively uninvolved, but there is some delta.  Slowly, by 11:23:22, there may be some spread into the strip that is above 11, 12.  The nurse enters at 11:25:58 and interacts with the patient again.  The seizure starts to slow in the same pair of contacts 11 through 15 and then seems to almost stop at 11:27:38, has a burst of epileptiform activity, and then there is profound slowing particularly in that strip 11 through 15 with some involvement in the lateral temporal strip as well and the LM mesial grid in contacts 8 through 10 and 13 through 15. After the spiking, delta is picked up at LM8, 9; 9, 10; 14, 15. subject: aaaaaewf
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: This is a 13-year-old male with aspiration pneumonia and a tonic-clonic seizure with eyes rolling back in the head lasting four to five minutes.  Patient was admitted to hospital 10/07/06.
MEDICATIONS: Antibiotics, Ibuprofen.
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG is performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG. Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.  This is an awake and drowsy record.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: In wakefulness, there is a 10 Hz, 50 uV posterior dominant rhythm.  The overall background is somewhat disorganized for age.  In this sharply contoured background, in the waking record, there is a subtle underlying approximately 3 Hz spike and wave activity.
Features of sleep include rhythmic background slowing, attenuation of the alpha rhythm, and an increase in beta activity.
The major abnormalities are seizures, the first of which occurs just prior to the start of 3 Hz photic stimulation.  The seizure is characterized by very high amplitude, frontally predominant 3.5 to 5 Hz spike and slow wave activity.  The seizure begins just as the tech starts photic stimulation.  The child is able to sit up when the tech calls his name, but he is not able to respond and motions after the discharge is over that he does not recall the code word.  A second discharge is identified, which begins during photic stimulation at 19 Hz and slightly outlasts the duration of the photic stimulation, with a total seizure duration of 18 seconds, again with high amplitude, symmetric frontally predominant spike and slow wave activity.  Photic stimulation is repeated, but additional seizures are not recorded.
HR:  84 BPM.
IMPRESSION: Abnormal EEG due to:
Two clinical seizures characterized by frontally predominant 3 to 5 Hz spike and slow wave activity with clinical impairment of awareness.
The interictal EEG demonstrates an occipitally predominant spike and slow wave discharge.
Mild degree of background disorganization.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: This EEG supports a generalized mechanism for epilepsy.  The seizures themselves were frontally predominant and relatively characteristic of absence epilepsy, however, the interictal discharges in the background were not so typical.  Mitigating factors may include this childâ€™s acute medical illness.

subject: aaaaagnh
Clinical Report:
LENGTH OF THE RECORDING:  28 minutes.
CONDITION OF THE RECORDING:  The recording was performed according to the standard 10-20 system with additional T1 and T2 electrodes and a single lead EKG lead.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 58-year-old woman who presents with generalized tonic-clonic convulsions.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The posterior dominant rhythm consists of moderate amplitude polymorphic 8-9 Hz alpha activity that attenuates with eye opening.  There is an anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient that is at times unstable.  Additional diffuse polymorphic theta is captured.  Additional excess diffuse beta is observed.  During sleep phase, sleep spindles and K-complexes are observed.  Intermittent, primarily independent, and rarely synchronous bitemporal moderate to high amplitude sharp waves with after slow waves are captured over the anterior temporal regions.  Phase reversal occurs over F7/T1 and F8/T2 electrodes.  Left temporal epileptiform discharges are more present and more frequent.  Additional intermittent polymorphic theta and delta are captured over the anterior temporal regions.  Hyperventilation was performed with good effort and results in symmetrical diffuse background slowing.  Photic stimulation does not result in additional abnormalities.
FINDINGS:
Mild diffuse slowing.
Excess beta.
Independent greater than synchronous bilateral anterior temporal sharp waves with after going slow waves with phase reversal over F7/T1 and F8/T2 electrodes.  No electrographic seizures.  Additionally, intermittent bitemporal mild to moderate focal slowing.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal awake and asleep 28-minute EEG due to mild diffuse slowing, intermittent mild to moderate bilateral anterior temporal slowing, and intermittent left greater than right independent and synchronous bilateral anterior temporal epileptiform discharges.  Diffuse background slowing indicates etiologic and nonspecific diffuse cerebral dysfunction.  Focal bitemporal slowing and bitemporal epileptiform discharges indicate focal cerebral dysfunction, with increased epileptogenic potential, structural in etiology.  Excess beta could be secondary to medication effect such as benzodiazepines and barbiturates.

Occasional moderate amplitude isolated diffuse frontally predominant sharply contoured slow waves without clear spike waves or sharp waves are captured.  A good example of such waveform occurs at 09:59:50 a.m.  On a single occasion at 10:01:20 a.m., such discharge is correlated with a brief generalized body jerk. 1. Mild diffuse slowing.
2. Runs of non-evolving frontocentral theta activity.
3. Isolated diffuse frontally predominant slow waves that on a single occasion correlates with the generalized body jerks.

IMPRESSION:  This is a very abnormal EEG due to mild diffuse slowing, runs of sharply contoured bifrontocentral activity and occasional diffuse waveforms without clearly formed sharp waveforms that on a single occasion are associated with brief generalized body jerks. Focal slow-waves seen at F8/T4, seen frequently with a frequency of 2-4 Hz and an amplitude of 20-40 microvolts.

Right anterior temporal focal slow waves.
Mild generalized slow waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals no evidence of focal cerebral dysfunction in the right anterior temporal region and mild diffuse cerebral dysfunction,
both of which are nonspecific with regards to etiology.  No seizures or epileptiform discharges are clearly seen.

No stage II sleep.
No epileptiform discharges.
Rare sinus bradycardia.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal awake and drowsy 25-minute EEG.  No epileptiform discharges were captured, however stage II sleep was not recorded. If clinical indication for seizures persists, a repeat study with stage II sleep may be of benefit.

subject: aaaaaifp
Clinical Report:
LENGTH OF THE RECORDING:  22 minutes.

CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 73-year-old man with a past medical history of seizures, dementia and hepatitis C.  He is now admitted with acute respiratory distress.  On 05/26/2013, he was found to be unresponsive with generalized tonic clonic convulsions.

MEDICATIONS:

CONDITION OF THE RECORDING:  The recording was performed at the patient's bedside.  The recording was performed according to the standard 10-20 system with additional T1 and T2 electrodes and a single EKG lead.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not performed.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORDING:  During maximal wakefulness the posterior dominant rhythm reaches 8 to 8.5 Hz activity.  There is an anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient that is weak.  There is small polymorphic theta and delta captured.  During drowsiness the background is replaced by diffuse theta mixed with delta activity.  Frequent polymorphic theta and delta activity are captured over the left hemisphere, more so over the central leads.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not performed.

FINDINGS:
1. Mild to moderate diffuse slowing.
2. Intermittent focal left hemispheric slowing, most pronounced over the central leads.
3. No clear epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures.

IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal awake and drowsy 22 minute EEG due to mild to moderate diffuse slowing that is more pronounced over the left hemisphere.  Diffuse slowing indicates etiologically nonspecific diffuse cerebral dysfunction.  Focal left hemispheric slowing is suggestive of focal cerebral dysfunction, possibly structural in etiology.  The lack of epileptiform discharges on a routine EEG does not preclude a clinical diagnosis of epilepsy.

subject: aaaaaimz

INTRODUCTION:  A routine EEG was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with the addition of anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded while awake and lethargic.  Activating procedures were not performed.

Overall the background consists of delta frequency, slowing seen diffusely with some variability up on stimulation.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal sharp waves seen frequently at 01 and at times at T5/P3.  Frequently throughout the recording at times the sharp waves become rhythmic and occurred at a frequency of 1-2 Hz and then they start and stop.
Focal slowing seen in the posterior quadrant in the area of 01/T5/P3.  This slowing at times is rhythmic but does not  clearly evolve or appear to be epileptic.
Diffuse slowing seen at a frequency of 2-4 Hz, amplitude 20-60 microvolts.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to left occipital and posterior temporal parietal sharp waves as well as almost continuous focal slowing and generalized slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with the diagnosis of localization related epilepsy with seizures likely arising from the left occipital or posterior temporal parietal region.  In addition, there is evidence of focal cerebral structural dysfunction in the left posterior quadrant, which may be related to the previous hemorrhage.  Lastly, there is evidence of diffuse cerebral dysfunction, which is nonspecific with regards to etiology and may be due at least in part to medication effect.  No seizures were seen.

subject: aaaaajqo
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 66 year old woman status post recent cardiac stent placement with progressive encephalopathy and asterixis. Seizures this am, now intubated with right arm twitching.

MEDICATIONS: Ativan, Phenobarbital

INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. The recording begins at 9 a.m. after the patient has had 8 mg Ativan. She is intubated and demonstrates intermittent rhythmic movement of the right hand only. This was a 20 minute baseline and 5 hour EEG.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: Although there Is a fair amount of muscle and motion artifact in the EEG, when the muscle artifact drops out, a lower voltage pattern of a mixture of theta and delta activity can be identified. There is almost a bit of discontinuity with 2 Hz delta activity and scant amount of 6 Hz theta activity. Superimposed upon this is a periodic complex from the right hemisphere maximum in the right frontotemporal and frontocentral regions with an F2 sharp wave.

Stimulation of the patient produces muscle artifact. Some sections of the record with more muscle artifact are associated with eye movement and unfortunately in these sections of the record, it is difficult to really define the underlying EEG pattern due to the muscle artifact.

As the recording continues, the epileptiform activity can be identified particularly using an average reference and it is maximum more at F8 than at FT2. Again, each time that the patient has eye movement, it is more challenging to see the EEG, but the eye movements are relatively slower.

Phenobarbital is initiated at 10:54 for a total of 500 mg. This actually produces an increase in overall theta frequency in the record and more of a transition to a more of a rhythmic theta pattern. There are some sharp waves picked up in the left frontal region at 11:58. There is a subtle intermittent discontinuity noted particularly at 2 p.m. The sharp waves are improved despite some muscle artifact as the recording continues. It is not possible to determine if the patient has been twitching later on in the record. Her hand is covered up. By 3:14 when the record stops and she seems to have a little bit more muscle artifact, the sharp waves can be identified but they are not occurring in bursts. The EEG concludes at 3:18.

IMPRESSION: This is a challenging but markedly abnormal EEG due to:
1. An initial clinical appearance of seizure characterized by movements of the right hand and repetitive sharp waves in the right.
2. Marked background slowing.
3. Very slow pattern seen initially with an improvement to more of a theta frequency pattern following the use of the phenobarbital.
4. Rare frontal sharp waves on the left.

CLINICAL CORRELATION: Overall, by the end of this section of this EEG, there is an improvement in the overall background, which is particularly important given the significant focal features noted initially.

INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG was preformed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. During the course of the record, the patient's phenobarbital reaches 50. She has occasional clinical seizures twitching In her right hand and eye blinking.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The background EEG varies from a very low voltage suppressed pattern to 1 with a bit of theta and then a sharp wave in the right hemisphere with a generous field of spread. The activity at this point seems to start at T1 with a complex field of spread and it is primarily in the left hemisphere. As the recording continues including into the early morning hours on the 5th, there is some intermittent asymmetric discontinuity, but in fact as the phenobarbital level, moves toward 50, there is a bit more of a rhythmic background.

An electrographic seizure occurs at 5:36 a.m. The video camera is on the left of the patient and the patient's left arm jerks, but the other side cannot be identified. The activity begins at 53602 and must be localized to the left hemisphere. The activity continues and the patient's right face can seen to be jerking at 5:37. A clinician is at the bedside and walks away during the event, but then returns and provides oral hygiene during the seizure.

Following the seizure, the sharp waves are noted primary in the left hemisphere with rhythmic theta and some sharp waves at F8. Additional seizures are picked up by the seizure spike detector. subject: aaaaalgi
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 43 year old male with acute hydrocephalus, left temporal lobe intracerebral hemorrhage in June, 2010, left craniectomy in 2010, followed by stroke. Patient was admitted January 6, 2011, with craniotomy defect, confusion, DVT, and stroke.
MEDICATIONS: Keppra, Lasix, Metoprolol, K-Dur
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. The patient is awake.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: In wakefulness, the background EEG is somewhat disorganized. Despite a breach rhythm, there is a well-defined left anterior temporal sharp wave noted in wakefulness. Other features of this record include bursts of paroxysmal sharply-contoured slowing out of the left temporal region which occur without a clear clinical correlate. Because of the prominent eye-blink, attempt is made to reduce this. No clinical features are seen in association with the eye-blink artifact.
Following this, the patient becomes drowsy. There is an increase in background slowing. In drowsiness, the epileptiform activity demonstrates a more extensive field of thread, other anterior temporal or centroparietal in nature. No additional paroxysmal bursts of slowing are noted.
HR: 84 bpm
IMPRESSION: Abnormal EEG due to:
1. Sharp waves identified from the left temporal region which may be anterior temporal, centroparietal, or posterior temporal.
2. Bursts of paroxysmal slowing in the left temporal region without clinical correlate.
3. Epileptiform activity in the left mid-to-anterior-temporal region as well as posterior temporal region.
4. Generalized background slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: This EEG suggests a prominent, epileptogenic or epileptiform process on the left. No clinical seizures were recorded. Ongoing vigilance is appropriate.

subject: aaaaalib
Sleep patterns: vertex wave, K-complexes, spindles and POSTS
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  49 year old right handed woman with epilepsy since age 12. The patient continues to have 3 nocturnal seizures per week. Complicated history of diabetes, hypertension, aortic valve repair, migraine headache and heart problems since birth requiring multiple surgeries.
MEDICATIONS:  Topamax, Phenobarbital, Dilantin, Digoxin, Coumadin, Diovan, Bumex, ASA, Toprol
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed.  This is an almost exclusively sleep record.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The background EEG includes a stage I and stage II sleep with frequent transitions between stage I and stage II. There are vertex wave, K-complexes, spindles and POSTS.  Some of the vertex waves are somewhat asymmetric. While most are maximum at CZ, they have a more generous field of spread and the diphasic wave is significantly higher amplitude and more prolonged on the left compared to the right. The composition of the wave forms, however, is quite similar with a 0.97 hertz after going slow wave. Brief wakefulness includes a 9.5 hertz alpha rhythm. Photic stimulation is performed while the patient is in stage II sleep and does not activate the record, significant snore artifact is noted.
HR:  N/A
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Asynchronous vertex waves with more prominent field of spread on the left in sleep.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This patient's sleep pattern with frequent transitions between stage I and stage II sleep and prominent snoring may be part of an underlying sleep disturbance and can contribute to the nocturnal seizures. The asynchronous sleep wave forms may be correlated with imaging. No definitive epileptiform findings were noted, however.

subject: aaaaalib
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  50 year old right handed female with a history of atrial fibrillation, congenital heart disease, obstructive sleep apnea, hypertension, diabetes, and epilepsy since age 12, described as tonic-clonic seizures during sleep occurring frequently.
MEDICATIONS:  Phenobarbital, Dilantin, Toprol, Pantoprazole, Bumetanide, Lanoxin, Diovan, Metolazone.
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video baseline routine EEG was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrodes.  The patient was recorded in during wakefulness and sleep.  Activating procedures included hyperventilation and photic stimulation.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a posterior dominant rhythm that at best reaches 9 Hz which is reactive to eye opening.  The patient is recorded in wakefulness and sleep, stage I and stage II.  Activating procedures include hyperventilation with which she had a poor effort and did not reveal any abnormalities, and photic stimulation which caused no abnormalities.  Please note that the patient was mainly in sleep during the recording and there appeared to be an excessive beta seen diffusely throughout the recording.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:  Focal slow waves seen intermittently at F7/T3 and symptoms at T3 at a frequency of 2 to 4 Hz and amplitude of 20 to 60 microvolts.  Please note, there was right frontal and left frontal single focal slowing seen in the recording once at F4 and F3 independently.
SEIZURES:   None
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Left anterior temporal slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of left anterior temporal focal cerebral dysfunction, which is nonspecific in regard to etiology.  No seizures or epileptiform discharges are clearly seen.

subject: aaaaalim
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 62 year old female with metastatic rectal cancer, complex partial seizures.
MEDICATIONS: Dilantin, Keppra, Ativan, Versed.
INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. The patient is described as lethargic. On the morning of 3/8/2011, her mental status remains poor and she is having difficulty communicating as well as handling her secretions. With one channel of EKG. Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The background EEG is somewhat challenging due to artifact but demonstrates high amplitude right occipital parietal or occipital temporal spike and slow wave activity and polyspike and slow wave activity. The polyspike activity is seen in the posterior temporal and parietal regions. The activity from the right hemisphere includes a progressively slow and featureless pattern. After the administration of phenytoin at 19:08, the right hemisphere demonstrates progressive impairment with epileptiform activity over a suppressed background. The left hemisphere is moderately slow with a theta frequency pattern. There is some volume conduction from the epileptiform activity on the right to the left. The bursts of fast activity in the right occipital region persist. On some occasions throughout the night, muscle artifact can be seen in association with the bursts and on other occasions, the
epileptiform activity is not uniformly associated with contralateral left facial twitching. Sleep and wake patterns are identified with sleep identified at 23:43 and a much slower pattern seen from the left. The epileptiform activity persists with brief bursts and build up in sleep.
By 7:50 in the morning, the patient is more arousable. The epileptiform activity is clustering in brief bursts. Two mg Ativan is given at 8:55. Of note, in the morning, prior to the administration of Ativan, the patient is moving about, using her right side purposefully an attempting to communicate despite the epileptiform activity from the left.
HR: 90 bpm
IMPRESSION/CLINICAL CORRELATION: This section of the EEG is compatible with an ongoing epileptiform process. Of note, intermittently the activity is increasingly suppressed on the right hemisphere which is worrisome for cerebral pathology.

subject: aaaaalim
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 62 year old female with metastatic rectal cancer, complex partial seizures.
MEDICATIONS: Dilantin, Keppra, Ativan, Versed.
INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. The patient is described as lethargic. On the morning of 3/8/2011, her mental status remains poor and she is having difficulty communicating as well as handling her secretions. With one channel of EKG. Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The background EEG is somewhat challenging due to artifact but demonstrates high amplitude right occipital parietal or occipital temporal spike and slow wave activity and polyspike and slow wave activity. The polyspike activity is seen in the posterior temporal and parietal regions. The activity from the right hemisphere includes a progressively slow and featureless pattern. After the administration of phenytoin at 19:08, the right hemisphere demonstrates progressive impairment with epileptiform activity over a suppressed background. The left hemisphere is moderately slow with a theta frequency pattern. There is some volume conduction from the epileptiform activity on the right to the left. The bursts of fast activity in the right occipital region persist. On some occasions throughout the night, muscle artifact can be seen in association with the bursts and on other occasions, the
epileptiform activity is not uniformly associated with contralateral left facial twitching. Sleep and wake patterns are identified with sleep identified at 23:43 and a much slower pattern seen from the left. The epileptiform activity persists with brief bursts and build up in sleep.
By 7:50 in the morning, the patient is more arousable. The epileptiform activity is clustering in brief bursts. Two mg Ativan is given at 8:55. Of note, in the morning, prior to the administration of Ativan, the patient is moving about, using her right side purposefully an attempting to communicate despite the epileptiform activity from the left.
HR: 90 bpm
IMPRESSION/CLINICAL CORRELATION: This section of the EEG is compatible with an ongoing epileptiform process. Of note, intermittently the activity is increasingly suppressed on the right hemisphere which is worrisome for cerebral pathology.

subject: aaaaalim
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 63 year old female with multiple seizures.
MEDICATIONS: Keppra, Dilantin, ASA, Ciprofloxacin
INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG. Due to refractory status epilepticus the patient receives multiple medications, including versed drip, followed by phenobarbital. She develops hypotension with the use of versed. She is intubated and poorly response.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The EEG monitor continues to demonstrate repetitive spike and polyspike activity in the right posterior quadrant, including C4, T6, with spread to T4. The activity can occur in brief bursts and runs. There is some volume conduction to the left. Intermittently the pattern includes a burst suppression pattern. The activity is reviewed up until 2:00p.m. and again the polyspike activity and burst suppression pattern is noted.
Of note, although the pattern persists, by the overnight recording, with medications employed there is a much clear transition to a burst suppression pattern, particularly on the right. The left hemisphere clearly demonstrates the impact of the anesthetic medications by 2:00p.m. with a much slower pattern.
HR: 60-104 bpm
IMPRESSION: There is no conclusion because we are still recording, but the interval impression is ongoing, refractory focal status epilepticus with a burst suppression background.
LONG TERM MONITORING 11-26-03/1312011 TO
INTRODUCTION: Continuous digital video EEG monitoring was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. The patient is intubated and essentially comatose.
MEDICATIONS: Phenobarbital, Dilantin, Keppra, Lacosamide, Vimpat
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: Despite the patient's immobility over the course of the EEG monitoring sessions, many of the electrodes are lost. The initial sections of the record include a very suppressed pattern on the right with intermittent spike and wave activity. In this section of the record, the spike and wave activity is occurring as infrequently as once every 20 seconds. The background on the left is mixed frequency slow pattern but the right hemisphere is essentially a suppressed pattern particularly in the right temporal and posterior quadrant. There is high amplitude polyspike or fast activity noted.
On the morning of 3/14/2011, as the patient's medications are adjusted, the background is a bit more vigorous in both hemispheres and the epileptiform activity starts to increase to pairs with bursts lasting less than a second but is mainly one burst every 6 to 7 seconds. The left hemisphere demonstrates an increase in the vigorous pattern with a mixed frequency pattern.
HR: 96 bpm
INTERVAL IMPRESSION/CLINICAL: This day the EEG monitoring is remarkable for an initial pattern with very suppressed and infrequent epileptiform activity followed by activity which is seen in brief isolated bursts but no build up. Additional EEG monitoring will follow.

subject: aaaaalim
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 63 year old female with refractory status epilepticus following stroke. Rectal carcinoma.
MEDICATIONS: Dilantin, Phenobarbital, Zofran, Pantoprazole, Insulin, Vimpat
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. The patient is intubated and somnolent.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The left hemisphere includes a mixture of theta frequency activity with some arrhythmic delta activity and some beta. The right hemisphere demonstrates a relatively suppressed background particularly in the posterior temporal region. The frontocentral regions demonstrate some mixed frequency activity. The major abnormality in this record is a high amplitude left occipital spike and slow wave complex which is seen as frequently as 5 discharges in 20 seconds but sometimes is infrequently as only 2. This is a very high amplitude irritative focus but it no longer evolves.
HR: 90 bpm
IMPRESSION: Abnormal EEG due to:
1. Prominent high amplitude right occipital spike and slow wave complex.
2. Right occipital temporal suppression of cerebral activity.
3. Moderate background slowing from the left.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: This EEG is markedly improved and the patient is no longer having seizures and the epileptiform activity is isolated and it no longer revolves. These findings are congruent with the MRI which demonstrates a significant right TCA stroke.

INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG. The patient is ventilated.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD
The overall background is very low voltage,
featureless, and has elements of a discontinuous  pattern with a relatively low voltage, suppressed pattern lasting for 2 to 3 seconds and then bursts of theta.  Reviewing the video, it is not possible to determine if motor activity accompanies the low voltage bursts of mixed theta, delta.  The bursts of mixed theta, delta are typically 1.5 to 2 seconds in duration.  There is some variability in the record in that some sections of the record
have a bit more delta.   Stimulation of the patient does not produce a significant change in the background.
HR: 	96 bpm
IMPRESSION:
Markedly abnormal EEG due to:
1. 		Replacement of normal background with what is essentially a discontinuous pattern.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: These are worrisome findings 11 days post cardiac arrest. There were no clinical seizures. 	If there are additional questions or concerns, additional neurodiagnostic testing may be helpful to more completely evaluate this individual's prognosis and to correlate this with the individual's cerebral function.

subject: aaaaalof
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 46 year old right handed woman with possible seizure
characterized by episodes with loss of balance, loss of speech, slurred speech, memory impairment, but no incontinence. The last episode was April 30, 2011.
MEDICATIONS: None.
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG was performed in lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG. Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: In wakefulness, there is a 7 Hz posterior dominant rhythm. With testing, the posterior dominant rhythm remains somewhat slow at 6-7 Hz. Hyperventilation is performed early on in the record. It produces some head rocking, but a suggestion of right posterior slowing. Lambda waves are noted in wakefulness. Features of stage 1 sleep include an increase in beta, POSTS, and then spindles in
stage 2 sleep. Photic stimulation is performed as the patient is drifting in and out of stage 2 sleep. There is no significant driving response.
HR: 90bpm
IMPRESSION: Abnormal EEG due to:
1. Background slowing with a slow alpha rhythm.
2. Question of a focal hyperventilation response with a subtle of focal slowing on the right.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: The head rocking artifact of hyperventilation makes the focal features a bit more difficult to interpret. The alpha rhythm is slow in this individual. Medications or a mild diffuse disturbance of cerebral function can contribute to slowing of the alpha rhythm.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 51 year old right handed woman, with past nocturnal tonic clonic
seizures. Seizure-free for many years. The patient had an episode earlier this year
with right sided numbness which she describes as much different than the seizure she
had many years ago. She has hyperlipidemia.
MEDICATIONS: Aspirin.
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG was performed in lab using standard 10-20
system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG. Hyperventilation and photic
stimulation are performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: In wakefulness there is a relatively low voltage
background with symmetric, reactive 10 Hz alpha rhythm. Drowsiness is characterized
by an increase in theta. There is prominent eye movement artifact. There is a scant
asymmetry noted in the record in hyperventilation. In hyperventilation there is a slight
asymmetry with a very small amount of theta noted in the left temporal region in
comparison to the right. There is also a very slightly asymmetric photic driving
response. Although there is a fair amount of artifact, there is a single, left temporal
sharp wave observed. Wicket activity is also noted on rare occasions. Photic
stimulation otherwise elicits symmetric driving response.
HR: 72 bpm with occasional PVCs.
IMPRESSION: Very mildly abnormal EEG due to:
1. Subtle abnormal hyperventilation and photic driving response.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: There is a small amount of wicket. In that the left
temporal region, sharp wave is only identified on one occasion this must be considered
artifactual. If there are additional episodes of right sided numbness, additional EEG
recording is warranted.

subject: aaaaalqm
Clinical Report: CLINICAL HISTORY:  52 year old right handed woman with history of seizures and she has been seizure free for 19 years and migraines that presents with an episode of vomiting and dizziness as well as feeling unsteady.  MEDICATIONS:  Meclizine REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures. INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded in wakefulness and sleep.  Activating procedures including photic stimulation were performed and hyperventilation as well. TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None. DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a well-defined posterior dominant rhythm that reaches 9 Hz which is reactive to eye opening.  There is an increased amount of frontocentral beta.  Activating procedures including photic stimulation and hyperventilation produced no abnormal discharges. ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:  None. SEIZURES:  None. IMPRESSION:  Normal awake and sleep EEG. CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No seizures or epileptiform discharges were seen.

subject: aaaaalug
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 81 year old woman with recurrent slurred speech, confusion and fever.
MEDICATIONS: Dilantin
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. Little other clinical data is available.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: As the tracing begins, the patient is intubated. The background includes a medium amplitude, mixed frequency pattern rich in beta and some alpha frequency activity. The patient is moving about intermittently and there is some true artifact. Stimulation of the patient produces initially an attenuation of the background and then some theta frequency activity. There is some variability in the record. As the recording continues, the attenuation is seen a bit more commonly but in addition, stimulation of the patient also sometimes elicits muscle artifact.
HR: 90 bpm
IMPRESSION: Abnormal EEG due to:
1. Replacement of normal background with a mixed frequency pattern.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: These findings support a diffuse disturbance of cerebral function, but no other conclusions can be made given the absence of a clinical history.

subject: aaaaalug
Clinical Report:
LENGTH OF RECORDING:  23 minutes.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 51-year-old woman with a history of right MCA stroke and bipolar disorder as well as seizure disorder.  She now presents with being found unresponsive on the floor.  She has approximately 1 seizure a month.
MEDICATIONS:  Depakote.
CONDITION OF THE RECORDING:  The recording was performed according to the standard 10-20 system with additional T1 and T2 electrodes and a single EKG lead.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not performed.  The recording was performed at bedside.  Some of the electrodes had high impedances due to poor patient cooperation throughout the recording.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  There is a right to left interhemispheric asymmetry with faster frequencies observed over the left hemisphere.  The posterior dominant rhythm over the left hemisphere consists of polymorphic moderate amplitude 8.5 to 9 Hz alpha activity.  The posterior dominant rhythm over the right hemisphere consists of polymorphic low to moderate amplitude 7 to 8 Hz theta.  The anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient is weak bilaterally.  No normal sleep architecture is captured.  Additional diffuse polymorphic theta is observed.  Intermittent left hemispheric sharp waves are captured.  Phase reversal occurs over F7, T1, T3 and Fp1, F3 electrodes.  There is nearly continuous EMG artifact that effects variable leads.  Premature QRS complexes are captured on a single EKG lead.
FINDINGS:
Mild diffuse slowing.
Mild to moderate right hemispheric slowing.
Intermittent sharp waves over the left frontal/anterior temporal region.
Occasional premature QRS complexes seen on EKG.
EMG artifact that is nearly continuous effecting variable leads throughout the recording.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal awake only 23 minute EEG due to mild diffuse slowing, mild to moderate right hemispheric slowing, as well as intermittent left frontal/anterior temporal epileptiform discharges.  Diffuse slowing indicates etiologically nonspecific diffuse cerebral dysfunction.  Focal right hemispheric slowing indicates focal cerebral dysfunction, likely structural in etiology, and is consistent with the patient's history of right MCA territory stroke.  There is an epileptogenic focus over the left frontal/anterior temporal region.  A large amount of EMG artifact is present throughout the recording, so subtle abnormalities might have been missed.  No clear electrographic seizures are captured.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  74 year old right handed man with hypertension and end-stage renal disease, CAD, an ICH in 2010, prostate cancer, presented with change in mental status.
MEDICATIONS:  Benadryl, Keppra, Ativan.
REASON FOR STUDY:  Change in mental status.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded during wakefulness and drowsiness as well as lethargy.  Activating procedures were not performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  Some movement artifact throughout the recording.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely slow background with a posterior dominant rhythm that at best reaches 7 Hz.  The patient is recorded in wakefulness and drowsiness with some stage changes, but no normal sleep architecture is seen.  No activating procedures were performed.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:  Generalized slow waves at 5-7 Hz with an amplitude of 20-60 microvolts seen continuously.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Mild-to-moderate generalized slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of mild-to-moderate diffuse cerebral dysfunction, which is nonspecific with regards to etiology.  No seizures or epileptiform discharges were seen.

subject: aaaaalzg
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 42 year old right handed male with new onset seizure activity. Evaluate for alcohol-withdrawal seizures 4/3/2011.
MEDICATIONS: Serax, Keppra, Ativan
INTRODUCTION: Digital video EEG was performed in lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG. Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed. This is an awake through stage 2 sleep in a very drowsy individual.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD: The overall alpha rhythm is relatively low voltage, 12 Hz. Rhythmic temporal theta of drowsiness characteristic 5-6 Hz is identified in the transition to sleep. Vertex waves and spindles are noted. The patient continues to have eye movement into stage 2 sleep. Hyperventilation produces an increase in amplitude of the background. Photic stimulation is performed while the patient is falling back into stage 2 sleep, but does elicit scant driving.
HR: 72 bpm
IMPRESSION: This EEG is within normal limits.
CLINICAL CORRELATION: There are a number of normal variants in the EEG, including rhythmic temporal theta of drowsiness. The overall background is very low voltage and there is generous beta. The generous beta may be due to the use of benzodiazepines. The low voltage background is not pathological in and of itself.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  43 year old right handed male presents with seizures.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, Depakote, Ativan
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG is performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG.  The patient was markedly drowsy after a number of seizures, treated with Ativan.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The majority of the EEG includes stage 2 sleep with vertex waves, spindles, K-complexes, and POSTS. Photic stimulation produces a driving response discernable in stage 2 sleep.  There is some head rocking movements noted.
HR:    90 bpm
IMPRESSION:  Normal EEG primarily in sleep.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This is the second normal EEG for this individual.  If further information is needed to characterize this patient's epilepsy, EEG monitoring may be necessary.

subject: aaaaalzg
Sleep patterns: K complexes and sleep spindles, and POSTS
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 44-year-old man with a past medical history of adult onset seizures who presented with 2 episodes of generalized tonic-clonic seizures.  The patient remained lethargic.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, Ativan, and others.
INTRODUCTION:  A digital video EEG monitoring was performed using the standard international 10-20 system of electrode placement and 1 channel of EKG monitoring.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  Records were daily reviewed for EEG abnormalities and clinical events, wakefulness, stage 2 sleep, and a slow wave sleep were captured.  There was an automated spike and seizure detection system in place as well as the patient activated alarm system.  The patient was drowsy with short periods of wakefulness during this recording.
Interictal EEG:  In the most alert state, there was a posterior dominant rhythm with a frequency of 9-9.5 Hz and 25-40 microvolts seen in the occipital region, which attenuated with eye opening.  There was presence of low-amplitude diffuse beta frequency, which is distributed symmetrically.  Drowsiness, stage 1 and a stage 2 sleep were seen during the most part of this recording.  Stage 1 sleep was characterized by the disappearance of the alpha rhythm slowing of the background and absence of eye blink and muscle artifact, vertex sharp waves, and POSTS.  Stage 2 sleep was characterized by the presence of K complexes and sleep spindles, and POSTS.  The deeper stages of sleep have characterized by increasing amounts of delta frequency activity.  There were no epileptiform activities or seizures were captured during this recording.  Heart rate was 90 to 110 beats per minute.
CLINICAL EVENTS:  No clinical events were captured during this recording.  The patient was drowsy, but arousable by calling the patient.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal EEG monitoring study capturing short periods of wakefulness and for the most part of the recording drowsiness stage 1 and stage 2 sleep.  There were no epileptiform activities or seizures noted during this recording.  This EEG does not rule out the diagnosis of epilepsy.  If seizures are of a clinical concern, a repeat prolonged video EEG monitoring may be helpful.  Please note that the patient was tachycardiac.

subject: aaaaalzg
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 44-year-old male with refractory epilepsy, presented to the emergency room after seizure at home, had another seizure in the emergency room and then a complex partial seizure in front of the resident at 8:00 p.m.  The patient remains poorly responsive.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, Protonix, Depakote.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient was quite somnolent during the EEG and had a seizure.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  As the tracing began, the background EEG was quite slow and disorganized.
Interictal epileptiform discharges were identified with low amplitude sharp waves seen primarily in the right temporal region.
A seizure was identified shortly after the start of the EEG around 2:06 a.m. lasting approximately 1 minute.  This was characterized by eye flutter.  The patient was on the video and it is a bit hazy but the technologist described eye flutter and the video also seems to generate an arousal.  The first seizure out of the right is about 1 minute long and as the epileptiform activity evolves and slows a second seizure lasting approximately 20 seconds is noted out of the left.  This is associated with much higher amplitude slow delta and it ends abruptly.  During the left temporal seizure the patient has repetitive jerking of the trunk that occurred during the second seizure.  The first seizure was at 2:19.
Low amplitude intermittent left temporal sharp waves were noted to follow this.
No additional seizures were noted on the baseline EEG.
Heart rate 102 bpm.
IMPRESSION:  This is a markedly abnormal EEG due to:
Marked background slowing.
A right temporal lobe seizure.
A second seizure which localizes to the left and seems to evolve just as the seizure out of the right ends.
Marked background slowing.
Heart rate reaching 150 bpm during the second seizure out of the left.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is diagnostic of the localization related epilepsy.  The clinical history with onset of seizures before 8 a.m., failure to awaken and the presence of this seizure on the EEG raises concerns about a later form of status epilepticus.
