subject: aaaaalzg
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 44-year-old male with refractory epilepsy, admitted to the unit with repetitive seizures.  He had 2 tonic-clonic seizures and was admitted for persistent postictal obtundation.
MEDICATIONS:  Keppra.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient was somnolent and uncooperative.  Impedances were challenging.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The background EEG is diffusely slow, but with generous beta activity, particularly in the central regions.  Stimulation of the patient produces muscle, a brief alerting, and some theta as well as transient alpha activity, but then the patient drifts back to sleep.
Heart rate 66 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Excessive beta.
Generalized background slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No epileptiform features were identified.  This EEG is most compatible with postictal state and/or medication effect.  No epileptiform features were identified.  The background EEG is similar to a previous postictal EEG recorded a month ago, but low voltage temporal spikes were not identified today, and no complex portal seizures were recorded.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 44-year-old male with adult onset complex partial epilepsy, refractory, tonic-clonic, and secondarily generalized medicines.
MEDICATIONS:  Depakote.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were completed.  This is a technically satisfactory routine EEG.
DESCRIPTION OF RECORD:  In wakefulness, the background EEG is low voltage, but there is an 8.5 Hz posterior dominant rhythm with a generous amount of low voltage, frontal central beta.  Alpha squeak was noted.
Stage II sleep includes vertex waves, spindles, K complexes, and POSTS.
Hyperventilation produces an increase in amplitude of the background.  Photic stimulation was performed while the patient was in stage II sleep and did not significantly activate the record.
Heart rate 66 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  Low voltage, otherwise normal EEG.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No epileptiform features were identified in the routine EEG.  Additional EEG recording was undertaken to characterize this patient's epilepsy.

Clinical correlation is recommended.  No seizures or epileptiform discharges were clearly seen.  These does not exclude the possibility of epilepsy or of intermittent seizures.

subject: aaaaamhb
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 22-year-old female with a history of seizures after a stereotactic frontal craniotomy for a central neurocytoma and VP shunt, now with recurrent seizures, low Dilantin levels.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin 1 gram, Ativan 12 mg, as well as added Ativan afterward; later intubated and placed on propofol.
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video long-term EEG for 18 hours was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional EKG and anterior temporal electrodes.  The patient was comatose and unresponsive during the EEG.  No activating procedures were performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffuse background which is disorganized, mainly in the delta/theta frequencies with no normal posterior dominant rhythm and no normal frontocentral beta.  No normal sleep architecture is also seen.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges seen at T4-T6 at a frequency of 2 Hz with amplitude of 20 to 75 microvolts.  These discharges are seen continuously but appear to evolve as described in the seizure paragraph.  During the recording, these PLEDs become more blunted and less frequent, but they are continuously seen.  At times during the last part of the recording, they appeared to have more anterior predominance, moving from T4 forward to the F8 region.
Diffuse slow waves seen in the recording.
SEIZURES:   Frequent seizures are seen emanating from the T6-T4 electrode which evolve from PLEDs and stop.  There is a contralateral left hemisphere artifact as well as in the video left arm trembling can be seen during these seizures.  There are multiple of these occurring at the beginning of the recording, almost continuously. However, as the over time the recording improves, the periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges become more blunted and the seizures stop.  During the rest of the EEG, the last 10 hours of the EEG, there is only intermittent muscle artifact on the left hemisphere but no clear actual seizures being seen.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Frequent seizures arising from the left posterotemporal region, which later improve, and none seen during the last 10 hours.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with the diagnosis of localization epilepsy arising from the right posterotemporal region, with multiple seizures seen from that region.  Please note that the seizures improve and none are seen during the last 10 hours. In addition there is continuous evidence of acute focal dysfunction and irritability in the right hemisphere.  Lastly, there is evidence of diffuse cerebral dysfunction which in this patient is likely due, at least in part, to medication affect.

subject: aaaaamhb
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  23 year old woman with brain tumor, craniotomy, VP shunt, admitted with recurrent status epilepticus.
MEDICATIONS:  Keppra, Dilantin.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG.  The patient is awake.  Seizures are noted during the EEG.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  As the tracing opens, the patient is awake.  The EEG activity from the left hemisphere is, in fact, quite normal with a 9 Hz alpha rhythm.  An alpha rhythm is also identified on the right, but there is a breach rhythm, medium to higher amplitude, arrhythmic delta activity and a sharp wave which is seen out of the breach rhythm, which may be maximum at T2 or T4.  There is also some periodic slowing noted.
The patient's first seizure occurs at 10:29:21, 6 minutes into the EEG.  She starts to pick or fuss with her covers.  She looks to the left.  She is a little bit interactive with the tech and the tech asks her to count to 10, but by the time the tech is asking her to count to 10, the seizure is over.  Total seizure duration is about 20 seconds.  Characteristics of this seizure include rhythmic, repetitive right temporal theta frequency activity and some postictal slowing.  Eye movement artifact is also prominently picked up in the temporal electrodes.
At the close of the record, as the nurse is working with the patient, an additional burst of the prominent arrhythmic delta activity is noted on the right.  The patient is awake and it is not clear that this event was considered clinically significant.
HR:    120 bpm
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
A brief, well-defined seizure out of the right temporal region.
Focal slowing in the right temporal region.
Another burst with prominent periodic delta also out of the right.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is diagnostic of a focal mechanism for epilepsy.  Previous EEGs demonstrated T6 seizures and PLEDs.  Today's seizures are a little bit more anterior.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY: 22 year old female, status post craniectomy for central neurocytoma removal and radiation, as well as VP shunts, who presents with recurrent seizures.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, Keppra, Depakote.
REASON FOR STUDY:  Recurrent intractable seizures.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video long term monitoring EEG was performed for 24-hours using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded in wakefulness and sleep.  Activating procedures were not performed.  This record was reviewed using continuous spike detection, as well as seizure detection monitor and the patient was noted with no events and nurses notes.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a well defined posterior dominant rhythm that reaches 9 Hz and has a slightly higher amplitude over the right hemisphere.  There is normal frontal central beta with a slight increase also in the right hemisphere.  No activating procedures were performed.  Sleep architecture can be seen.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
* Focal sharp waves seen infrequently at T4, C4.
* Focal slow waves seen continuously at T4/C4 at a frequency of 2-4 Hz with an amplitude of 20-60 mV with an associated breech in that region.
EPISODES:  The patient had throughout the recording about 8 or 9 episodes in which she would press the alarm and slightly start turning her head side to side without clear loss of consciousness, lasting for more than 20-30 minutes.  During this event the patient may have been given saline solution or was asked to please stop it and she could control stopping the shaking motion.  During this episode the EEG remained unchanged from the prior baseline and no seizure activity was noted.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
* Right central temporal slowing.
* Sharp waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with a diagnosis of localization related epilepsy with seizures likely arising from the right central temporal region.  No epileptic seizures were seen.  During the recording also multiple events of head shaking were captured with no EEG correlate.  As such these episodes are nonepileptic and are suggestive of the diagnosis of psychogenic nonepileptic events.  Lastly, the EEG did reveal evidence of focal cerebral structural dysfunction, which is consistent with the known surgery and craniotomy on the right central region.  Again, no seizures were seen.

ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal slow waves seen over the right hemisphere maximal in the T4 electrode at 2-4 Hz, amplitude of 20-70 microvolts.
Generalized slowing at 2-4 Hz with an amplitude of 20-60 microvolts seen continuously throughout the recording.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Right hemispheric slowing maximal in the midtemporal region along with an associated breach in that region.
Generalized slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of focal structural dysfunction in the right hemisphere maximal in the midtemporal region which is consistent with the known surgery in that region. No seizures or epileptiform discharges were clearly seen.

Right hemispheric slowing, maximal at the mid temporal region.

subject: aaaaamhb
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a young woman with previous cranial resection, transferred from an outside hospital with recurrent seizures and CT scan abnormality, now awake.  The patient was reported to have had two simple partial seizures the night before.
MEDICATIONS:  Phenytoin (stopped), Keppra, Depakote, Baclofen, famotidine and others.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside in the ICU using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  This was an awake and drowsy record in a relatively drowsy adult.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In brief wakefulness, the activity from the left hemisphere is reasonably well organized with a glimpse of a 9 to 10 Hz alpha rhythm with a small amount of background data on the left.  The record is relatively low voltage on the left.  The right hemisphere demonstrates continuous arrhythmic delta activity, a breech rhythm with higher amplitude sharply contra delta.  There are occasional sharp waves noted in this background, including T6, T4, P4.
With stimulation, the patient clearly produces a response.
Heart rate is 66 bpm.
IMPRESSION:
This is an abnormal EEG due to:
1.	Continuous focal slowing from the right hemisphere.
2.	High amplitude irregular delta of the right hemisphere.
3.	Occasional rhythmic slowing from the right hemisphere.
4.	Sharp waves noted in the right mid to posterior temporal region, as well as parietal region.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:
This record is similar to previous studies.  The activity from the left is not as slow as previous occasions.  No clinical seizures were noted.

subject: aaaaamic
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  84 year old woman with subdural hematomas and relatively abrupt lethargy and aphasia.
MEDICATIONS:  Keppra.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG.

There are occasional bursts of left midtemporal sharp waves which can be broadly contoured.  They may be maximal at T1, T3 or F7.  There are also substantial portions of the record without sharp waves.  Arrhythmic delta is prominent in the left frontotemporal region.
HR:    78 bpm
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Generalized background slowing.
Focal slowing from the left temporal region.
Left anterior temporal sharp waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No seizures were recorded.  This EEG supports a focal mechanism for seizures.  If there are additional concerns a follow up EEG may be of value.

HR:    72 bpm.
IMPRESSION:  Normal EEG.

INTRODUCTION:  Continuous video EEG was performed in the unit using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient recalls 3 episodes.  The first is on the 19th at noon where she felt a little startled.  The second was at 10 p.m. where she felt a mini aura characterized by tingling sensation on her face.  She did not recall an episode at 5:25, although she did push the button for a tingling sensation.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  Random wakefulness and sleep includes a well-organized 10 Hz alpha rhythm with a small amount of low-voltage frontocentral beta activity.
Event time 11:22:  There is a burst of rhythmic left temporal theta delta with frequency evolution.  This is 5 seconds in duration.  There is no push button at that time.
Event time 5:25:20 p.m.:   A burst of rhythmic theta, frequency was subtle, frequency evolution lasting only 3 seconds.  Thirty seconds later the patient reports the mini-aura which she no longer recalled.
Event time 9:58 p.m.  No abnormalities on the EEG were identified at that time.
Event time10:25 a.m.:  There was another burst lasting 4 seconds in duration in the morning on the 20th which is similar to the 2 prior bursts.   No clinical features were recalled.
HR:    60 bpm
INTERVAL IMPRESSION:  There are 3 bursts of rhythmic theta from the left temporal region with frequency evolution.  These are concerning as a potential interictal epileptiform discharge.  They may also be clinically significant with the mini-auras that the patient experienced.
LONG TERM MONITORING - # 12- 72 â€“ 06/20/2012 TO 06/21/2012

The data is reviewed for the clinical symptoms that the patient experienced, but no epileptiform features are identified.
HR:    66 bpm
LONG TERM MONITORING - # 12-72 â€“ 06/21/2012 TO 06/22/2012
INTRODUCTION:  Continuous video EEG monitoring was performed using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel EKG.  The patient reported an aura at 8 p.m. on the 21st.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In wakefulness, there is a 10 Hz alpha rhythm.  Around 8 p.m., the patient is noted to transition in and out of stage 2 sleep, waking up and falling back asleep.  Fragments of deeper stages of sleep are noted overnight.
No electrographic seizures were identified.  The seizure and spike detector did not identify any other epileptiform abnormalities.
Event time 11:55:  The patient pushes the button at 11:56:12 stating that she feels as if she is about to have an aura.  She puts her hand to her chest and later appears to be tearful.  The EEG is awake at this time.  No epileptiform features were noted.   For her 8:00 p.m. the patient reported symptoms that her throat was closing with the seizure.

HR:    60 bpm
IMPRESSION:  Continuous digital video EEG monitoring session was remarkable for:
3 bursts of rhythmic, paroxysmal theta, all less than 4 seconds in duration, but with frequency evolution.

On one day, however, there were discharges which appear epileptiform in nature.  While this patient did not have any of her larger spells with head turning, a feeling as if her throat was closing or impairment of awareness, this record does not exclude a diagnosis and clinically, diagnosis may be considered.

INTRODUCTION:  A routine EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead  EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded while confused, unresponsive, and lethargic.  Activating procedures were not performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely slow background with a posterior dominant rhythm that reaches 5-7 Hz and there is some frontal central beta.  No normal sleep architecture is seen.  Activating procedures were not performed.
Abnormal discharges:
Frequent T5 sharp waves seen throughout the recording.  At times the sharp waves would suddenly become rhythmic and continue for about 2-3 minutes and then stop.  They did not clearly evolve, but became extremely arrhythmic throughout this time.
Focal slowing seen at T5 at a frequency of 2-4 Hz at an amplitude of 20-60 mV.
Moderate diffuse slowing at 5-7 Hz seen continuously throughout the recording.
Seizures:  As above mentioned there is bursts of T5 sharp waves that did not clearly evolve, but would start and stop suddenly, at times for minutes with no clinical correlate.  These are concerning for possible electrographic seizures.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to left posterior temporal sharp waves and monomorphic discharges concerning for electrographic seizures, in addition to generalized slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with a diagnosis of localization related epilepsy with seizures likely arising from the left posterior temporal region.  Note:  The bursts of rhythmic sharp waves were concerning for electrographic seizures, as they had no clinical correlate.  In addition, there is evidence of moderate diffuse slowing, which may due at least in part to medication effect.  These findings were reported to the primary team who decided to treat the patient and will place the patient on long term monitoring.

subject: aaaaamoa
Clinical Report:
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 46-year-old woman with first episode of seizures and a known left occipital AVM.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin and Keppra.
INTRODUCTION:  A long-term EEG was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with addition of anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded during lethargy and confusion.  No activating procedures were performed.   Continuous spike detection software as well as seizure detection software and nurses' notes as well as patient alarms were used to review the EEG.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  During the last 8 hours of the recording, there was multiple electrode artifact as they were detached.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record remains without background which has a very seldom posterior dominant rhythm that reaches 7-8 Hz.  There are no activating procedures and there is some frontal central beta seen.  Overall, however, is mainly diffusely slow and a theta 5-7 Hz frequency.  Some sleep architecture can be seen.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal sharp waves seen continuously at T5 at a frequency of 1-2 Hz in a monomorphic fashion consistent with periodic lateralizing epileptiform discharges (PLEDs).
Focal slow waves are seen over the left hemisphere, maximal at T5 continuously throughout the recording at a frequency of 2-4 Hz and amplitude of 20-60 microvolts.
Generalized slow waves at 5-7 Hz is seen throughout the recording continuously.
SEIZURES:  The patient had multiple electrographic seizures that would appear to arise from the left hemisphere, maximally over the posterior temporal region at T5 intervals and a delta rhythmic discharge that increases in frequency and amplitude and becomes better formed until it suddenly stops.  This first seizure occurred at 10:43:18 a.m. and ended at 10:45 a.m. A second similar seizure occurred at 4:21:57 p.m. and ended at 4:25:51 p.m. and the last seizure occurred around 10:50 p.m.  At that point, multiple electrodes had been lost which made it difficult to evaluate the origin of the seizure but appeared to be similar to the other ones.  Please note that during none of these seizures there appeared to be a clinical correlate.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal electroencephalogram due to left hemispheric seizures arising possibly from the left posterior temporal region in addition to left posterior temporal periodic lateralizing epileptiform discharges and focal slow waves in addition to generalized slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with the diagnosis of localization related epilepsy with multiple seizures arising from the left hemisphere, likely posterior temporal region.  In addition, there is evidence of acute structural  in the left posterior temporal region.  Lastly, there is evidence of diffuse cerebral dysfunction which is nonspecific in regards to etiology.  These findings were continuously reported to the primary team to make adjustments in treatment based on the seizures.

subject: aaaaamoa
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 46-year-old woman, admitted with increasing seizure frequency.  Her seizures are felt to be secondary to AVM.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin and Keppra.
CONDITION OF THE RECORDING:  The recording was performed according to the standard 10-20 system with an additional T1 and T2 electrode and a single EKG lead.  Seizure detection software was activated.  Nursing notes and medical staff notes were reviewed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  There is significant right to left interhemispheric asymmetry, with slower waveforms over the left hemisphere.  During maximal wakefulness, the background over the right hemisphere consists of a mixture of polymorphic low to moderate amplitude theta and delta without expected posterior dominant rhythm or anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient.  The background over the left hemisphere consists of a mixture of polymorphic low to moderate amplitude theta and delta activity with additional polymorphic delta over the right temporal region.  No posterior dominant rhythm or anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient is observed over the left hemisphere.  During sleep, sleep spindle-like complexes are captured.  Continuous moderate amplitude sharp waves with after-going slow waves are captured over the left posterior temporal region.  Intermittently throughout the recording, these discharges occur rhythmically at approximately 1 to 2 Hz and correspond to periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges.  Approximately 8 electrographic seizures are captured.  They start with increasing frequency epileptiform discharges, and rapidly evolve into rhythmic theta and later alpha, most prominent over the left posterior quadrant.  As seizure progresses, these discharges spread throughout the left hemisphere and decrease in frequency to about 3.5 Hz delta range.  These seizures last approximately 3 to 4 minutes.  There is no clinical correlate that is evident from the video review.  From approximately 4 p.m. until approximately 5:30 p.m., several leads are intermittently disconnected.  This limits interpretation of the EEG during that time period.
FINDINGS:
Focal left hemispheric slowing.
Frequent left posterior quadrant epileptiform discharges that intermittently organize into 1 to 2 Hz PLEDS.
Approximately 8 electrographic seizures that start over the left posterior quadrant and spread throughout the left hemisphere.  These seizures do not have a clear clinical correlate.
IMPRESSION:  This is a very abnormal 24-hour continuous audio-video EEG due to:
Moderate diffuse slowing and disorganization.
Focal left hemispheric slowing.
Frequent left posterior quadrant epileptiform discharges that are frequently periodic.
Approximately 8 electrographic seizures lasting 3 to 4 minutes with onset over the left posterior quadrant.
Diffuse slowing indicates nonspecific cerebral dysfunction that in this case could partially be related to the medication effect.  Focal left hemispheric slowing and epileptiform discharges indicate structural pathology over that region that has high epileptogenic potential.  There is an epileptogenic focus over the left posterior quadrant.  When compared to the EEG from the prior 24 hours, there has been no significant change in the degree of the background slowing or frequency of seizures or epileptiform discharges.  These findings were conveyed to the primary team.

subject: aaaaamuy
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  73 year old male, status epilepticus.
MEDICATIONS:  Ativan, Versed, Valproic Acid
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG.  The patient has increased axial tone, stiffening, and poor responsiveness.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  This is an extremely challenging bedside ICU record.  There is a great deal of muscle and movement artifact.  The overall record is high amplitude, sharply contoured, with what seems to be some T6 sharp waves and a sharply contoured background with arrhythmic delta activity.  There is a relative paucity of any of the normal wave forms of wakefulness and sleep.  In the initial sessions of the record there is some activity which can be reviewed and the activity seems to be slower with more high amplitude sharp waves on the left than the right.
Because of these technical issues, after the first 20 minutes the patient receives versed and Benadryl and an additional recording is initiated at 17:31.  This is a total of 40 minute EEG.
This section of the record again is somewhat challenged by muscle and motion artifact.  There seems to be a T6 triangular shaped sharp wave in this record.  In addition, towards the later sections of the record, as the muscle activity drops out, there is an asymmetry, particularly at the end of the record with periodic sharp activity in the left hemisphere.  In addition, there is a significant asymmetry with significantly less faster frequency activity on the left than the right.  There are also some periodic complexes at 01, noted in later sections of the record, but no definitive seizures.
HR:    100 bpm
IMPRESSION:  This EEG is remarkable for:
A markedly abnormal background.
Epileptiform activity from either hemisphere and shifting slowing.
A pattern at the close of the record which demonstrated primarily epileptiform activity in the left hemisphere, some of which was posterior, some of which was anterior.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG can be seen in the context of a postictal or late ictal record.  Because of the predominance of features on the left in a patient with structural abnormalities, primarily on the right, additional EEG recording was requested.

subject: aaaaamuy
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  73 year old right handed man with history of diabetes, alcohol abuse, and subdural hemorrhage with a craniotomy in 1980, presents with severe weakness, episodes of seizures, last episode was about a month ago.
MEDICATIONS:  Simvastatin, ASA, Tamniosm (?)
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded in wakefulness and drowsiness.  No activating procedures were performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a posterior dominant rhythm that can only be seen over the right hemisphere that at best reaches 8 Hz with an amplitude of 20-60 microvolts, which is reactive to eye opening.  There is a lack of frontocentral beta over the left hemisphere, but is clearly seen over the right.  No normal sleep architecture is seen.  No activating procedures were performed.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal slow waves seen over the left hemisphere maximal at P3/T5 at 2-4 Hz with an amplitude of 20-70 microvolts.
In addition, there are focal sharp waves at P3/T3 and T5 intermittently.
In addition, mild generalized slow waves at 5-7 Hz in wakefulness seen mainly over the right hemisphere.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Left hemisphere slow waves.
Sharp waves maximal in the parietal and posterotemporal region.  There is an associated breach in this region.
Generalized slow waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with diagnosis of localization-related epilepsy with seizures likely arising from the left parietal and posterotemporal region.  There is also evidence of focal cerebral dysfunction in this region, which may be due at least in part to the known subdural and craniectomy in the past.  Lastly, there is evidence of mild diffuse cerebral dysfunction, which is nonspecific with regards to etiology.  No seizures were seen.

Generalized background slowing.

subject: aaaaamuy
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  73 year old right handed man with a history of subdural resection and seizures, transferred from an outside hospital with seizures, receiving 8 mg of Ativan and Dilantin.  Questionable CT from outside hospital with right subdural and midline shift.

MEDICATIONS:  Ativan, Dilantin

REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.

INTRODUCTION:  Digital video long term monitoring  EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrodes.  The patient was recorded while unresponsive.  Activating procedures were not performed.

TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely slow background with no normal posterior dominant rhythm or normal frontocentral beta.  Activating procedures were not performed and no normal sleep architecture was seen.

ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
1. Focal sharp waves are seen in the left hemisphere, at times maximal in the F7 region with a slight lag, finishing with maximality at O1 at a frequency of 1 to 2 Hz with an amplitude of about 40 to 70 microvolts, consistent with periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLED's).  These PLED's do not evolve and maintain rhythmicity throughout the recording.
2. Focal slowing in the right anterior temporal region at F8/T4 at a frequency of 2 to 4 Hz with an amplitude of 20 to 60 microvolts and associated increasing faster frequencies consistent with a breach.
3. Generalized slow waves at 2 to 4 Hz seen throughout the recording.

SEIZURES:  None.




IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
1. Left hemispheric PLED's.
2. Right anterior temporal focal slowing and breach in the setting of generalized slow waves.

CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This long term EEG reveals evidence of focal structural dysfunction irritability in the left hemisphere which is consistent with an acute structural lesion.  In addition, there is evidence of focal cerebral dysfunction in the right anterior temporal region which is consistent with the known surgery.  No seizures were seen.

Markedly attenuated activity in the left hemisphere.
Complex epileptiform activity, much of which is in the posterior quadrants, sometimes at P3, but sometimes in the left frontal region or in the right occipital region.
Moderate slowing from the right.

MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, phenobarbital, and Keppra.
INTRODUCTION:  A long-term EEG was performed for 48 hours, using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single-lead EKG electrodes.  The patient was recorded in wakefulness and sleep.  Activating procedures were not performed.  Continuous spike detection software as well as seizure detection software along with patient alarms were used for reviewing the EEG.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely slow background with no normal posterior dominant rhythm and/or frontocentral beta.  There is no normal sleep architecture.  No activating procedures were performed.

Focal sharp waves were seen in multiple places including F8/T2, F7/T3, T5, C4, and P3.  In addition to this, there are bursts of polyspike and wave at 2 Hz seen intermittently throughout the recording on both days.  There is no clinical correlation to these polyspike and wave discharges.
At times there were, during the first 24 hours more frequently than the last 24, discharges of fast beta frequency discharges with an abrupt ending seen at T5/P3 and at times at P4/T6 with no clear correlate.  They would last for about 10 to 15 seconds and then stop.

During this, the EEG would suddenly attenuate and there would be a fast beta pattern seen diffusely followed by muscle artifact.  During this recording, the patient had only 3 of these in the past 2 days.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to multifocal spikes, diffuse slowing, some electrographic seizures, and some clinical seizures.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of diffuse cerebral dysfunction, which is nonspecific with regard to etiology.  In addition, there is evidence of multiple focal irritability and diffuse seizures consistent with generalized symptomatic epilepsy.  Please note that this recording has markedly improved since the beginning and the clinical seizures have almost subsided and only 3 were seen in the past 48 hours.  These findings were reported to the primary team.

MEDICATIONS:  Lamictal, Keppra, Depakote, Dilantin, Xanax.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel of EKG.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In wakefulness, the background EEG is at times poorly organized and bilaterally slow.  In other sections, there is a more significant asymmetry with a pattern on the right that is only mildly slow and an alpha rhythm of 8 Hz on the right.  The alpha rhythm tends to be slower on the left, and there is disruption of faster frequency activity on the left with occasional sharp waves.  Features of drowsiness include more of a bilateral background slow pattern and shifting slowing. Photic stimulation is performed while the patient is confused.  There is scant driving on the right.
HR:    72 bpm
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Focal slowing from the left with disruption of faster frequency activity and arrhythmic delta activity.
Occasional sharp waves in the left hemisphere, particularly the temporal region.
Background slowing on the right which is variable, sometimes only moderately slow.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This is one of many EEGs for this individual.  She has previously been evaluated for repetitive seizures.  The right hemisphere may be a little bit slower than in the past, but the left hemisphere is not quite as remarkably slow.  These findings can be compared to the long-term monitoring and follow-up EEGs from May 2012.

subject: aaaaanog
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  An 82-year-old woman with metastatic cancer, cerebral metastasis found unresponsive, not able to move the right side and then had 2 tonic clonic seizures.
MEDICATIONS:  Currently unknown.
INTRODUCTION:  A digital video EEG was performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient was quite somnolent.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The background EEG demonstrates a diffusely slow pattern.  Superimposed upon this is arrhythmic delta activity from the left hemisphere.  Sharp waves are noted from the left hemisphere some of which are higher amplitude and broadly contoured at T3.  Other are more posterior central temporal.  Stage 2 sleep included vertex waves and spindles but the spindles are asymmetric.  Occasional bursts of finally predominant delta were observed.
Heart rate 90 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to:
Continuous arrhythmic delta activity from the left hemisphere.
Sharp waves in the left hemisphere with some variability including T3, C3 and T5.
Bihemispheric background slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  The bihemispheric slowing may be due to post ictal state or bihemispheric diffuse disturbance of cerebral function.  The focal slowing on the left and epileptiform activity may be correlated with imaging.  No seizures were recorded.

subject: aaaaanwn

subject: aaaaanzp
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 69-year-old man with a past medical history of seizures, depression, dizziness, hypertension, hyperlipidemia and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, who now presents with episodes of headaches.  Last seizure was approximately 1 month ago.
MEDICATIONS:  Current medications:  Dilantin, Ativan, citalopram, and others.
INTRODUCTION:  Condition of the recording:  The recording was performed to the standard 10-20 system with additional T1 and T2 electrodes and a single EKG lead.  Hyperventilation was not performed.  Photic stimulation was performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORDING:  The posterior dominant rhythm consists of low amplitude 10-11 Hz alpha activity that attenuates with eyes opening.  There is an anterior-to-posterior frequency amplitude gradient with faster frequencies and at lower amplitudes anteriorly.  During sleep vertex waves and sleep spindles are captured.  Hyperventilation was not performed.  Stepped photic stimulation resulted in no change in the background.  A single EKG lead demonstrates an irregular heart rate.
FINDINGS:
No epileptiform discharges.
Cardiac arrhythmia.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal awake and asleep 21 minute EEG.  The lack of epileptiform discharges on a routine EEG does not rule out a clinical diagnosis of epilepsy.  An irregular heart rate is captured on the single EKG lead.

subject: aaaaaock
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 24-year-old, left handed male, with epilepsy as a child, seizure free for 2 years, but recurrent seizures on October 15, 2012 characterized by right shaking for 1-2 minutes with tongue biting, loss of consciousness, and postictal confusion.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with  channel EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In apparent wakefulness, there is a glimpse of 9 Hz alpha rhythm, but there is excess theta.  Higher amplitude, triangular shaped, frontal delta was noted even prior to hyperventilation.
Features of drowsiness include slow rolling eye movements and attenuation of the high amplitude theta, as well as the bursts of FIRDA.  Bursts of higher amplitude delta were noted during photic stimulation and after photic stimulation, but without spike and wave activity.  Photic stimulation elicited a driving response at intermediate and faster flash frequencies.
Heart rate:  90 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Excess theta.
It seems to be frontally predominant rhythmic mixed theta delta.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No definitive epileptiform features were identified.  This EEG may hide an underlying frontal epileptiform focus.  A follow up EEG with cognitive testing is advised to more clearly determine when the patient is maximally awake.  In addition, more extended sleep may be helpful in capturing epileptiform activity.  The higher amplitude frontal slowing is not as apparent as the patient transitions into sleep.

subject: aaaaaocl
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 50-year-old male with epilepsy since age 12.  Subsequent cocaine dependence.  After a seizure, patient became violent.  Seizures characterized by an aura of strange feeling followed by postictal confusion.  Last seizure 1 day ago. The patient asked the technologist if photic stimulation would produce 1 of his typical seizures.  The technologist told him that, that was a possibility.  The patient subsequently had 1 of typical behaviors during photic stimulation, but after the photic stimulation had stopped.
MEDICATIONS:  Tegretol, Dilantin.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital EEG was performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were completed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In wakefulness, the background EEG is well organized with a 10 Hz alpha rhythm and a scant amount of low voltage, frontocentral beta.  Hyperventilation was performed early in the EEG and produces and increase an amplitude of the background.  Vertex waves and POSTS are observed in stage 1 sleep with brief stage 2 sleep.  The patient was falling asleep during photic stimulation and transitioning in and out of sleep during photic stimulation.
The patient had an event during photic stimulation.  The patient was drowsy prior to photic stimulations, the patient was waking up, although there were brief transitions in and out of sleep.
The patient was awake at the end of the 11 Hz stimuli, when the behavior occurred, he sat up abruptly.  His eyes were dilated, he was moving back and forth and his heart rate increased briefly to 150 BPM.
Although the audio was not working, the patient was able to communicate during the event.
There were no epileptiform features noted during the event.  No postictal features were identified.
Heart rate 78-150 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  The EEG is normal.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This patient had an unusual behavior.  The patient was clearly awake for several seconds prior to the event, but he had been drifting in and out of sleep.  The event had no epileptiform features, but a link either to a sleep-related disorder or a psychiatric illness may be considered.

subject: aaaaaocl
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  The patient is a 51-year-old man with the onset of seizures at the age of about 12 to 13 years of age.  In the last 20 years seizure semiology has changed.  Seizures are now characterized by an aura of strange sensations followed by loss of consciousness and postictal confusion.  During the events the patient has been told that he wanders.  He has multiple events per day, most of them are nocturnal.  Last such seizure was on 01/07/2013.
CURRENT MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin extended release 200 mg twice a day and Tegretol extended release 200 mg b.i.d.
CONDITION OF THE RECORDING:  The recording was performed according to the standard 10-20 system with additional T1 and T2 electrodes and a single EKG lead.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The posterior dominant rhythm consists of a well formed and modulated 9 Hz alpha activity that attenuates with eyes opening.  There is an anterior to posterior frequency amplitude gradient with faster frequencies at lower amplitudes anteriorly.  During sleep POSTS (positive occipital sharp transients of sleep), vertex waves and sleep complexes are captured.  Hyperventilation is performed with fair effort and produces no significant change in the background.  Stepped photic stimulation results in symmetrical occipital driving at multiple flash frequencies.
FINDINGS:  No epileptiform discharges to stage II sleep.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal awake and asleep 27-minute EEG.  No epileptiform discharges were captured.  The lack of epileptiform discharges on a routine EEG does not preclude a clinical diagnosis of epilepsy.

subject: aaaaaodl
Clinical Report:
TYPE OF STUDY:  Routine EEG awake and sleep.
DIAGNOSIS:  Rule out seizures.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 47-year-old right-handed woman with history of daily headache and memory problem, presents for near syncope, concerned for seizure.
MEDICATIONS:  The patient's medications include Topamax.
INTRODUCTION:  A digital EEG was performed in the laboratory using the standard International 10/20 system of electrode placement in addition to one channel of EKG monitoring.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed. This tracing captures wakefulness only.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In the most alert state alpha rhythm is 10-11 Hertz in frequency, 20-30 microvolt in amplitude seen in the occipital region which attenuates with eye opening.  There is a small amount of low amplitude frontocentral, beta activity which is distributed symmetrically.  Photic stimulation was performed at multiple flash frequencies and results in symmetrical driving response.  Hyperventilation was performed with diffuse symmetrical slowing.  There were no epileptiform abnormalities or seizures during this recording.  Heart rate was 90-95 beats per minute and regular.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal EEG recording capturing wakefulness only.  No epileptiform abnormalities or seizures were identified in this record.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  A normal EEG does not rule out the diagnosis of epilepsy.  If seizures are a clinical concern, a repeat EEG capturing deeper stages of sleep or 24-hour ambulatory EEG maybe helpful.

subject: aaaaaogc
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 48-year-old woman admitted on October 23, with fatigue and a fever found to have a right frontal subdural hematoma and a left MCA infarct.
MEDICATIONS:  None reported.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  This is an awake and drowsy EEG.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The background EEG is markedly abnormal.  Sections of the record include a rhythmic approximately 7 Hz theta frequency background with a 7-8 Hz alpha rhythm, but much of the record include significantly more slower rhythmic mixed theta and delta.  Superimposed upon this is some focal slowing with a bit more arrhythmic alpha on the left compared to the right and disruption of faster frequency activity.  In addition, occasional sharp waves are noted independently at T4, P4 and T1.  Sharp waves are relatively infrequent.  Stimulation of the patient produces an alerting response with an increase in the faster frequency activity.  Rare triphasic waves were observed.  Heart rate 97 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Generalized background slowing.
Intermittent, paroxysmal slowing.
Superimposed asymmetry with disruption of faster frequency activity on the left in comparison to the right.
Independent sharp waves seen from either hemisphere.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG supports a diffuse disturbance of cerebral function affecting gray and white matter.  In addition, a structural or functional process on the left should be considered.  There are infrequent sharp waves seen from either hemisphere.  These findings may even be correlated with imaging and clinical history.

subject: aaaaaogc
Clinical Report:
DIAGNOSIS:  Altered mental status.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 48-year-old female who was admitted for change in mental status.  On arrival, she was found to be aphasic.  She was confused, but responsive to her name.  Past medical history is significant for dilated cardiomyopathy, status post AICD and LVAD, LV thrombosis on Coumadin, atrial fibrillation, left MCA stroke, and peripheral neuropathy.
MEDICATIONS:  The patient's medications include Plavix, warfarin, ceftazidime, simvastatin, Ambien, Coreg, lorazepam, midazolam, and Topamax.
INTRODUCTION:  A digital EEG was performed at bedside using the standard international 10-20 system of electrode placement in addition to 1-channel of EKG monitoring.  This tracing captures patient in an obtunded stuporous state. Photic stimulation and hyperventilation were not performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In the stuporous state, the background activity was characterized by diffuse slowing consisting of sharply contoured delta wave slower on the left side with a frequency of 3-3.5 Hz high amplitude with sharp wave activities most prominent in the left central parietal and occipital regions There were also Triphasic waves seen intermittently with an Anterior- posterior gradient/ occurring with a frequency of 1-1.2 Hz.  Heart rate was 90 to 100 beats per minute.  Photic stimulation and hyperventilation were not performed.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG recording due to diffuse slowing with sharply contoured delta waves, and Triphasic waves and sharp wave discharges most pronounced in left posterior quadrant without evolution.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  Slowing of background activity and Triphasic waves are indicative of metabolic, toxic, hypoxic or infectious encephalopathy.  Alternatively it could be due to the effects of sedating medications or bilateral cerebral dysfunction.  The presence of sharp wave discharges are indicative of hyperexcitability of cerebral cortex that places this patient at risk for partial seizures.  Clinical correlation is recommended.

subject: aaaaaogc
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 48-year-old woman with a past medical history of heart failure status post LVAD and AICD, left ventricular thrombus, atrial fibrillation, left MCA stroke, declining mental status, and DNR-1 as of today.
MEDICATIONS:  Keppra, Topamax, __________, Lexapro, Coreg, and ceftriaxone.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside in the ICU using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient is intubated and poorly responsive.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  This EEG is markedly disorganized.  It is also sharply contoured.  The overall background includes rhythmic beta and triphasic waves.  Shifting slowing was identified.  In this sharply contoured background, focal sharp waves are identified in T4, C3, T1, and right posterior temporal.  Intermittent attenuations were noted in the background.  Stimulation of the patient did not activate the record.
Heart rate 102 bpm.
IMPRESSION:  This is a markedly abnormal EEG due to the following abnormalities:
Background slowing and disorganization.
Intermittent attenuation.
Triphasic waves.
Occasional isolated sharp waves seen from either hemisphere.
This EEG is similar to the prior EEG.  Toxic metabolic disturbances may accentuate the underlying pattern described above.

subject: aaaaaokm
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  52 year old female, status post code on January 11, 2012 with the development of myoclonus, treated with Ativan, versed, and Depakote.  Renal failure, status post dialysis on Friday.
MEDICATIONS:  Versed, Ativan, Depakote
INTRODUCTION:  Continuous digital video EEG was performed at bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG.  The patient is critically ill.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The initial sections of this 24-hour period includes some frontally predominant generalized periodic complexes and some triphasic waves.  There is little in the way of frequency evolution of these, although on the afternoon of January 13, 2012, prior to resumption of the EEG monitoring, the patient was reported to have a clinical seizure with eye deviation and focal facial twitching.
Over the course of this long term monitoring session, there is a significant reduction in the periodic activity or epileptiform appearing activity.
By the close of the record, a diffusely slow mixed theta pattern is noted.  This is a transition from a higher amplitude theta pattern with some frontal sharp waves.  There is some sections, including at 2:00 a.m., with frontal delta, but no eye movements are noted.
HR:    126 bpm
IMPRESSION:  This EEG demonstrates a dramatic improvement, most remarkable after 10:00 a.m. on January 14, 2012 with significant reduction of generalized periodic activity or some frontal slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This patient's post anoxic pattern is improving.  In addition, her epileptiform activity demonstrated marked reduction of her time.

subject: aaaaaoom
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 74-year-old right-handed woman status post cardiac arrest on 03/17/2013.  No CPR at home but intubated by EMS.  Critically ill patient.  Additional history not available but plan is for hypothermic protocol.
MEDICATIONS:  Fentanyl, Demerol, others.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital EEG was performed at the bedside in the ICU, using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient had not been paralyzed.  She had not achieved the hypothermic goal or hyperthermia had not been started at the time of the record at 16:05.
Hyperventilation and photic stimulation are performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  Background EEG demonstrates a burst suppression pattern with approximately 2 bursts every 20 seconds.  The bursts are 1 to 3 seconds in duration, and are associated with a little bit of jerking and oral facial movements.  In addition, in the period of time when the EEG is suppressed, following the bursts there is a very slow oral movement and then eye flicker, a bit of jerking, and oral bursts, and then the mouth opening and closing one more time while the EEG is suppressed.  Tactile stimulation does not activate the record.
The bursts of activity are high amplitude, almost with a 4Hz spike and wave-like burst and then a suppressed background.
Heart rate difficult to discern at times due to the background artifact, but 96 beats per minute.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Replacement of normal background with a burst suppression pattern.
Postanoxic myoclonus with the bursts with oral facial movements.
Absence of reactivity or significant variability.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  These are worrisome findings, more than 6 hours status post anoxic insult.  Due to this patient's cardiac condition, hypothermia protocol was initiated and additional EEG will follow.

Heart rate difficult to discern at times due to the background artifact, but 96 beats per minute.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to:
Replacement of normal background with a burst suppression pattern.
Postanoxic myoclonus with the bursts with oral facial movements.
Absence of reactivity or significant variability.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  These are worrisome findings, more than 6 hours status post anoxic insult.  Due to this patient's cardiac condition, hypothermia protocol was initiated and additional EEG will follow.

Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 74-year-old woman admitted on the 17th after cardiac arrest.
MEDICATIONS:
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside in the ICU using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient received hypothermic cooling, which was somewhat challenging in that the team was having difficulty getting the patient to the correct temperature.  The patient's temperature was not recorded on the continuous EEG.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  As the tracing begins, there is initially a burst of depression patterns, however, within the first 20 minutes of the recording, the EEG is becoming increasingly continuous, although there is a great deal of variability between a discontinuous pattern and a continuous pattern.  With continuity, there are generalized periodic complexes and fast activity.  Clinically, these seem to be associated with oral movements.
During later sections of the record, the patient begins to have oral automatisms even without the appearance of epileptiform activity, and by 5:40 p.m., there is a transition in that she is no longer consistently having any motor activity when she has the bursts of activity, although this is not a one-to-one correlate and sometimes has some oral movements when the EEG is low voltage and slow.
There are no clinical notes provided, but at 11:37, the patient is off camera.  The EEG is low voltage theta and then there is a burst of the periodic activity.  Over the course of the evening, the EEG pattern remains complex with different patterns including a burst depression pattern, another pattern with periodic delta with muscle to artifact, and another pattern with paroxysmal rhythmic theta.  Sharp waves and spikes were difficult to differentiate from muscle to artifact in later sections of the record.
Over the course of the night, Depakote 750 every 8 hours was added.
In the morning, 2 mg of Versed was given.
At the conclusion of the EEG, the background EEG is relatively continuous with underlying delta and some fast activity in the frontal regions.  There are, however, both muscle artifact, and generalized periodic complexes noted at 8:37, just prior to discontinuation of the EEG.  Looking at a glimpse of the patient through the device, the patient does appear to have clinical myoclonus with this jerking.
Heart rate 96 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  This is a markedly abnormal EEG due to:
Initial burst suppression pattern.
What may well represent multiple ictal patterns for this patient.
Post anoxic myoclonus with generalized periodic complexes.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is markedly abnormal and demonstrates multiple epileptic and anoxic features.  These are worrisome findings acutely post anoxic insult.  The findings were discussed with the clinical team.

subject: aaaaaoom
Clinical Report:
REASON FOR STUDY:  Status post PEA code.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 74-year-old woman status post cardiac arrest with no initial CPR administered and then intubated by EMS with a past medical history of hypertension.  This is a follow-up EEG to the one on the 17th for anoxic brain injury status post VFib.
MEDICATIONS:  Depakote and Versed.
INTRODUCTION:  A routine EEG was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with the addition of anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  Patient was recorded while unresponsive.  Activating procedures were not performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely suppressed background with intermixed antero maximal spike and wave which occur in bursts of 3 at a frequency of 1 Hz continuously throughout the recording.  There is no normal posterior dominant rhythm, no normal frontocentral beta or sleep architecture.  During each one of these bursts there is a time-locked EMG artifact consistent with a jerk in her jaw.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:  As described above.  Anterior predominant generalized spike and wave occurring in bursts of 3 spikes at times at a frequency of 1 Hz intermixed with complete suppression of the EEG.
SEIZURES:  None.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to anterior predominant spike and wave with intermittent suppression.
HEART RATE:  72, and appears to have an AV block.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of diffuse cortical dysfunction and irritability, and the intermittent generalized spike and wave with the associated jerks are consistent with myoclonus.  This seen in the setting of hypoxic injury forebodes a poor prognosis.  No seizures were seen.

subject: aaaaaooo
Clinical Report:
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 25-year-old man with a history of mental retardation, cerebral palsy, spastic quadriplegia, ventilator-dependent respiratory failure, seizure disorder, hypertension, dilated cardiomyopathy, hydrocephalus, and a history of encephalitis, now with recurrent seizures, rule out status.
MEDICATIONS:  Narcotics, zonisamide, primidone, benzodiazepines.  Will add the rest later.
INTRODUCTION:  A routine EEG was performed using the standard 10-20 electrode placement system with an anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  The patient was recorded while lethargic.  Activating procedures were not performed.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely slow background with no posterior dominant rhythm or frontal central beta.  There is no normal sleep architecture.  No activating procedures were performed.  Overall the background is of low amplitude consistent with delta slowing at 2-4 Hz at an amplitude of less than 20 microvolts seen continuously.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal sharp waves seen frequently at C44/F4 seen almost continuously throughout the recording.
Focal slow waves seen continuously at C44/F4 at a frequency of 2-4 Hz and an amplitude of 20-70 microvolts with additional asymmetric increased amplitude and faster frequencies consistent with a breach.
SEIZURES:  Throughout the recording, there were about 6 seizures which appeared to have an electrographic onset, sometimes at O2 and at times at C4 beginning with high-frequency beta discharge that could again start on the occipital region or the central region and increase in frequency and then in amplitude, later to slow down in frequency but increase in amplitude and would spread either from the occipital region towards the central region at C4 or from the central region to the occipital region and continue to evolve.  They would last for about 1 minute to 2 minutes.  Clinically, the patient appears to open his eyes and roll his eyes towards the right and may have some twitching of his arm and mouth movements.  The tech did question him once and he remained unresponsive.
HEART RATE:  120s between seizures and 134 while having a seizure.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to recurrent seizures as well as right central focal slowing and sharp waves.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals evidence of status epilepticus with multiple seizures arising either from the right occipital or central region.  In addition, there is evidence of focal cerebral, structural cerebral dysfunction and irritability over the right central and frontal region.  These findings were discussed with the primary team as soon as the EEG was read.  Long-term EEG will be performed.

Clinical Report:

MEDICATIONS:  Zonisamide, primidone, benzodiazepines, narcotics.
INTRODUCTION:  A long term EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode. The patient was recorded while unresponsive.  Activating procedures were not performed.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens with slow background with no posterior dominant rhythm or frontocentral beta.  There is no normal sleep architecture and no activating procedures were performed.  Overall, the background consists of low amplitude delta frequency slowing occurring continuously throughout the recording.
Abnormal discharges:
Focal sharp waves seen almost continuously over the C4 and CZ while in wakefulness and sleep.  There is also some O2 sharp waves seen intermittently.
Focal slowing at C4 CZ at a frequency of 2-4 Hz and 20-60 microvolts seen continuously when in increased amplitude compared to the left consistent with a bridged rhythm.
Generalized slow waves at 2-4 Hz amplitude of 20-30 microvolts seen continuously throughout the recording.
Seizures:  The patient continued to have frequent seizures that appear to arise mainly at CZ4/C4 with crisp beta discharge that appears to increase in amplitude and slow down in frequency, mainly remaining over the right central frontal region.  At times there appears to be spread to the right occipital region and at times it appears to frankly originate from the right occipital region at O2.  Clinically, the patient appears to have mouth movements and eye movements.  The seizures were very frequent at the beginning of the recording and appeared to slow down towards the last hour of the recording.  At the beginning there appeared to be 2-3 seizures per hour and towards the end there appeared to be 1 seizure per hour seen in the recording.
Heart rate:  144.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to multiple recurrent frequent seizures arising from the right central region and/or right occipital region, in addition to focal slowing and sharp waves from that region.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG is consistent with the diagnosis of status epilepticus with seizures arising from the right central region, in addition to the right occipital region.  In addition, there is evidence of focal structural dysfunction in areas that leaded from that same region.  These findings were reported to the primary team as they occurred.

subject: aaaaaotl
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 19-year-old male with epilepsy, seizure free for the last 4 years.  Previous EEGs.  He has hearing impairment and attention deficit disorder.  At age 15 he had 2.5 Hz spike and wave activity on an outside EEG, but by age 17 his EEG had become normal.
MEDICATIONS:  None.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were completed.  This is an awake and briefly drowsy record.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The EEG demonstrates an appropriate pattern for a young adult of this age with a 10 Hz alpha rhythm reaching 90 mV.  The alpha rhythm is sometimes a little higher amplitude on the left compared to the right.
Features of drowsiness include anterior spread of the alpha rhythm. Deeper stages of sleep were not sustained.  Posterior slow waves of youth were noted in wakefulness.  Although deeper stages of sleep were not achieved, just as the patient was becoming drowsy there seems to be some subtle underlying 4-5 Hz theta in the left occipital region.
Hyperventilation produces an increase in amplitude of the background.
Photic stimulation elicited a bilateral driving response.
Heart:  96 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  A very subtle abnormality was noted in this EEG just as the patient was becoming drowsy and included some focal theta in the left occipital parietal region.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No epileptiform features were identified.  For this individual with a history of early developmental pathology and possible meningitis, these findings may relate to that premorbid process.  The findings were seen in a very
restricted section of the EEG.  If there is any questions, additional EEG recording may be of value.

subject: aaaaaoui
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 55-year-old male with past medical history of right pericardial effusion, prostate cancer with prostatectomy, atrial fibrillation, who presents after a fall and head trauma at work.  At emergency department, had weakness, seizures.  Neuroimaging showed occipital parietal epidural hematoma, and scalp swelling.  Patient also had subdural hematoma, epidural hematoma, and subarachnoid hemorrhage.
MEDICATIONS:  Keppra, famotidine, Zofran, midazolam as needed, Dilaudid as needed.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were not performed.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The background activity is characterized by diffuse slowing consisting of theta activity with a frequency of 5-7 Hz intermixed.  Delta activity with a frequency of 3-3.5 Hz.  There is also occasional alpha activity with a frequency of 10-12 Hz.  There was right hemispheric slowing and attenuation throughout this study.  Eye blink artifact was noted occasionally, asymmetric with higher voltage on the left side.  There were occasional asymmetric sleep spindles noted with higher amplitude on the left hemisphere.  There were spontaneous variability and reactivity on the stimulation by EEG take.  No epileptiform activity or seizures were seen in this study.  Heart rate was 60-65 beats per minute with low amplitude QRS complexes.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG recording capturing patients in a sedated, lethargic state due to diffuse slowing of the background activity with hemispheric asymmetry characterized by right hemispheric attenuation and a slowing.  No epileptiform activity or seizures were seen in this study.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  Diffuse slowing and excessive theta are suggestive of metabolic, hypoxic, anoxic, or infectious encephalopathy.  Alternatively, it could be due to the effect of medications with bilateral cerebral dysfunction.  Right hemispheric attenuation and slowing could be seen in the structural lesions of the underlying cerebral cortex and in this case, extra-axial fluid collection and epidural hematoma.  Clinical correlation is recommended.  No epileptiform abnormalities are identified in this record.  No seizures were captured.  This does not rule out the diagnosis of epilepsy or of intermittent seizures.  If clinically indicated, a long-term EEG monitoring may be helpful.

subject: aaaaaoui
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 55-year-old male with history of anticoagulation status post fall with head trauma, right occipital, parietal and epidural hematoma, left parietal fracture, subdural hematoma, subarachnoid hemorrhage, post-traumatic seizure, status post 7 days prophylactic Keppra.  No additional seizures, numbness, weakness or tingling.
MEDICATIONS:  Tylenol, oxycodone.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  This was an awake and drowsy record with photic stimulation.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In maximal wakefulness, the EEG is reasonably well organized with a 10 Hertz alpha rhythm and a small amount of low-voltage, frontocentral beta.  There is scant theta in the frontocentral regions, right more so than the left.  There is a bit of shifting slowing and a bit more focal slowing on the right, as the patient becomes drowsy, but these are relatively subtle findings with a little underlying arrhythmic delta activity and some excess theta.  Vertex waves, K-complexes and spindles were noted in stage 2 sleep.
Photic stimulation elicited bilateral driving.
Heart rate 90 bpm.
IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal electroencephalogram due to:
Subtle focal slowing from the right, particularly frontocentral region.
A little shifting slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  No epileptiform features were identified.  The focal features described in this EEG are dramatically improved compared to a previous study, February 26.

subject: aaaaaovm
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is an adult with a history of closed head injury late 2012, significant bifrontal encephalomalacia, recently discharged from rehab but found to have a right temporal subdural hematoma.

MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, Keppra and Ativan.

INTRODUCTION:  Continuous video EEG monitoring was performed in the ICU using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  The patient was somnolent but intermittently agitated.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:

February 11- 12 is markedly abnormal.  Throughout much of the record, it is primarily a slow theta pattern on the right.  The left hemisphere demonstrates epileptiform activity with complex field of spread typically most remarkable in the left posterotemporal or parietal region but sometimes in the left frontal region as well.

Intermittent bursts in this periodic complex epileptiform activity were noted, sometimes with associated theta frequency activity and on 2 occasions at 9:30 and at 10:20 p.m. with a bit more sustained bursts.

The patient also had intermittent arousals although he was not particularly interactive.  After receiving 4 mg of Ativan for both of these, the overall background was significantly slower and the EEG transitioned more to a periodic complex in the left hemisphere with polyphasic activity and fast activity.  The overall background remained quite slow with intermittent bilateral attenuation.  Stimulation of the patient would produce an alerting response.

This first 24 hour section starts in the afternoon and by the morning of the 12th, there is an increase in faster frequency activity.  Sustained bursts are noted with some frequency evolution including at 9:40 a.m. but stimulation of the patient also produces an increase in faster frequency activity.

By 9:53 a.m. the patient is somewhat interactive with the nurse and intermittently attempts to get out of bed.  When he does so, there is a bit more faster frequency activity on the right but the epileptiform activity on the left really remains unchanged with a periodic pattern with some buildup but without definitive frequency evolution.

Additional doses of Ativan were given on the morning of the 12th, again producing attenuation on the right and faster frequency activity on the left.

Heart rate 96 BPM.

Events at 11:26 p.m. and 11:51 p.m. on the evening of the 11th were not associated with definitive epileptiform discharges and may have represented arousals.



FEBRUARY 12 THROUGH 13

INTRODUCTION:  During this 24-hour section of the EEG, the patient received Ativan, Keppra, Dilantin and Serax 60 mg three times daily.  The patient remained relatively obtunded, intermittently arousable.

DESCRIPTION OF RECORD:  The background EEG remains markedly abnormal with a relatively low voltage and slow pattern on the right.  The left hemisphere demonstrates a periodic complex with a relatively large field of spread, maximum at P3 T5, sometimes involving the left anterior temporal region.  It waxes and wanes, does not disappear and there is some buildup noted intermittently.

There are bursts of epileptiform activity which show some frequency evolution but without the characteristic start and stop of a typical epileptiform seizure, however particularly at 2:50 a.m. on the morning of the 13th, the pattern described above is certainly one of a late status epilepticus pattern.

By 8:00 a.m., the epileptiform activity seems to be waning a bit but remains continuous.  Only the periodic nature of it is slowing a bit.

A more clearly defined seizure is noted with more clear frequency evolution at 9:52.  Seizure onset is difficult to determine but there is clearly an increase in epileptiform activity.  No clinical signs are noted and this discharge seems to be stopping.  The seizure is about 3 minutes in duration but seizure on and offset is difficult to define.  Intermittent bursts of sharp waves and spikes are noted out of the left hemisphere in an otherwise bilaterally suppressed background without so much buildup.

Stimulation of the patient at 2 p.m. produces an increase in faster frequency activity in the background.

Additional subclinical seizures are noted at 2:21.  The patient is responsive during the seizures with eye opening and looking about despite the epileptiform pattern.

Heart rate 96 BPM.

IMPRESSION:  When reviewed at a slower paper speed, this EEG monitoring session is really most compatible with a late status epilepticus pattern.  Subclinical seizures were noted in an individual who is confused but actually responsive during the seizures.

Clinical Report:
REASON FOR STUDY:  Seizures.
CLINICAL HISTORY:  58-year-old man with bifrontal bur holes and evacuation of subdural hematomas with change in mental status, with the wife noting that he is lip smacking and staring but never returned to baseline.
MEDICATIONS:  Dilantin, Keppra, Vimpat.
INTRODUCTION:  A long-term EEG was performed using standard 10-20 electrode placement system with additional anterior temporal and single lead EKG electrode.  Patient was recorded while lethargic.  Activating procedures were not performed.  Continuous spike wave detection as well as seizure detection software as well as patient notes and nurse's notes and patient alarms were used to review the EEG.
TECHNICAL DIFFICULTIES:  None.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  The record opens to a diffusely slow background with no normal posterior dominant rhythm or frontocentral beta.  Overall, the recording appears to be mainly in the delta frequencies with some intermittent faster frequencies showing up periodically.  There are some stage changes noted.  No activating procedures were performed.
ABNORMAL DISCHARGES:
Focal sharp waves seen continuously at T5/P3 with a periodicity that ranges from 0.5 Hz to 2 Hz, at times becoming slightly less organized.  These are consistent with periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs).
Generalized slow waves at 2-4 Hz at an amplitude of 20-40 mV.
SEIZURES:  Although there were about 2-3 rhythmic runs of the PLEDs, there were 2 electrographic seizures.  One occurred at 4:15 p.m. lasting for a minute and a half.  The 2nd one occurred at 9:43 p.m.  Both of them did not appear to have any clear clinical correlate.  They were comprised of rhythmic P3-4/T5 sharp waves, evolving in frequency and amplitude and then suddenly stopping.
HEART RATE:  84.
IMPRESSION:  Abnormal EEG due to left posterior temporal and parietal periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) and electrographic seizures in addition to generalized slowing.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG reveals left posterior temporal and parietal seizures as well as a focus of irritability and cerebral dysfunction in that region.  This is likely related to the known lesion in that region.  In addition, there is evidence of diffuse cerebral  dysfunction, which is nonspecific with regards to etiology and may be due at least in part to medication effect.

subject: aaaaaowq
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 41-year-old female with history of multiple sclerosis, anemia, nephrolithiasis, and seizure disorders, who presents with increase in frequency of seizures.  The episode is described as eyes rolling up with foam at mouth lasting more than 20 minutes.  The patient also has the little spasms from multiple sclerosis.

MEDICATIONS:  Including Keppra and others.

INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG performed in the lab using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with one channel of EKG.  Hyperventilation and photic stimulation were performed.

DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  In the most alert state, the posterior dominant rhythm was 8-8.5 Hz in frequency seen in the occipital region, which attenuates with eye opening.  There is low amplitude beta activity in the frontocentral region, which is distributed symmetrically.  There is 5-7 Hz diffuse theta activity seen during wakefulness, which is distributed symmetrically.  There are frequent bursts of bitemporal slowing at F7/T3 and F8/T4 more prominent on the left side or independent left temporal artery slowing at F7/T3 during wakefulness and drowsiness.  Drowsiness is characterized by disappearance of posterior dominant rhythm, slowing of the background, and absence of muscle and eye blink artifact. There are bursts of frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA) with a frequency of 2-3 Hz. Heart rate was 100 beats per minute and regular.

IMPRESSION:  This is an abnormal EEG due to
1. Excess theta activity during wakefulness.
2. There is also bitemporal slowing, left more than right side seen during wakefulness and drowsiness.
3. FIRDA bursts. No epileptiform abnormalities or seizures were identified in this record.

CLINICAL CORRELATION:  The above findings could be due to toxic, metabolic, hypoxic, or infectious encephalopathy. Alternatively, it could be due to the effect of sedating medications, bilateral cerebral dysfunction, or degenerative brain disease.  The right temporal slowing is suggestive of either bilateral structural abnormalities or bilateral cerebral dysfunction. FIRDA bursts could also be due to a lesion in the deep subcortical frontal white matter or 3rd ventricle and has been at times associated with increased intracranial pressure. Clinical correlation is recommended. No epileptiform abnormalities or seizures were seen in this record.  This does not rule out the diagnosis of epilepsy or of intermittent seizures.  Seizures are clinical concern. A repeat sleep-deprived EEG or a long-term EEG monitoring at bedside maybe helpful.
