subject: aaaaaowq
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  Patient is a 41-year-old female with a history of multiple sclerosis and recurrent seizures.  The purpose of the long-term recording was to evaluate for possible recurrent seizures.  Her seizure semiology consists of generalized tonic-clonic movements with excessive salivation followed by unresponsiveness.  These events varied from 10-20 minutes in duration.  She also has intermittent leg spasms from multiple sclerosis that are of different semiology than these events.
MEDICATIONS: Keppra, baclofen, levothyroxine, nitrofurantoin, Percocet, Oxycontin, Xanax.
INTRODUCTION:  Continuous 24-hour bedside EEG monitoring was performed on this patient.  The EEG was performed using a standard 10-20 electrode placement with 1 channel EKG monitoring.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  During the recording epic, there were multiple stages of wakefulness and sleep capturing including wakefulness, drowsiness, and stage II-III sleep.  Activation procedures were not performed.
During the course of the recording, the heart rate was variable from 80-100 beats.  As the record opens, there is a symmetric posterior dominant rhythm of approximately 10-11 hertz that is reactive eye opening.  There is a small amount of frontocentral beta activity that was identified and is symmetrically distributed.  Throughout the recording, there are intermittent epic of stage I sleep characterized by reduction of the speed in the posterior dominant rhythm.
Central theta activity decrease in eye movements.  There is the appearance of stage II sleep that is heralded by symmetric vertex activity and were 14 hertz sleep spindles.  Throughout the recording, there is intermittent bursts of delta activity that correspond clinically on the video with episodes of clinical sleep. These appear consistent without the stage II or stage III sleep.  However, in between these events, there are intermittent epics in which there is rhythmic 2-3 hertz frontal delta activity that may either reflect sleep, but could also represent frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA).  Activation procedures were not performed during the recording.  At 9:25 a.m., there was a single patient denoted event.  On the video, the patient is seen to be sitting in the chair, having a conversation with the nurse and another person in the room.  At this point in time, she appears to be drowsy and at times has her eyes closed.  The background shows diffuse bi-hemispheric slowing in the theta and delta range and again this maybe consistent with clinical sleep.  There is no ictal discharges. Throughout the recording, there were no seizures detected. There were no interictal epileptiform abnormalities identified. Heart rate varied from 60-80.
IMPRESSION:  In summary, this is a continuous long-term monitoring bedside recording which demonstrates excessive amounts of clinical and electrographic sleep as well as episodes of intermittent rhythmic delta activity that may be consistent with FIRDA.  The presence of excessive clinical sleep may be suggestive of excessive sedating medications or sleep deprivation.  The presence of FIRDA suggests a lesion in the deep subcortical frontal white matter or 3rd ventricle and has been at times associated with increased intracranial pressure.  There were no ictal or interictal discharges identified in this record.  Further clinical correlation and monitoring is suggested.

subject: aaaaapkm
Clinical Report:
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 25-year-old woman who presents with history of new onset seizure-like activity.  Seizure is described as sudden onset of feeling bad smell or seeing colors or at times numbness and tingling in the left face and arm followed by loss of consciousness with no convulsion.  The episode usually last for a few minutes.  She also had, at times, she may experience staring spells lasting 25-30 seconds noted by her boyfriend.
MEDICATIONS:  Trileptal 300 mg b.i.d., vitamin D, Elavil, and Celexa.
INTRODUCTION:  A digital video EEG monitoring was performed in the EMU using a standard international 10-20 system of electrode placement and 1 channel of EKG monitoring.  Records were daily reviewed for EEG abnormalities and clinical events.  Sleep and wakefulness were captured.  There was an automated spike in seizure detection system in place as well as patient activated alarm system.
DESCRIPTION OF THE RECORD:  Interictal EEG description:  In the most alert state, there was a posterior dominant rhythm with frequency of 10-11 Hz, 30-80 microvolts in amplitude seen in the occipital region.  There was presence of low amplitude frontocentral beta activity, which was distributed symmetrically.  A stage 1 sleep was characterized by the disappearance of alpha rhythm, slowing of the background, and absence of eye blink and muscle artifact as well as the presence of vertex sharp waves and posts.  The stage 2 sleep was characterized by the presence of K complexes, sleep spindles.  A slow-wave sleep and REM sleep were also captured.  No interictal epileptiform abnormalities or evidence of focally slowing were noted.  Heart rate was approximately 70 to 90 beats per minute.
CLINICAL EVENTS:  On 08/08/2013, there was 1 pushbutton event during photic stimulation noted by technician at 1:21 p.m.  Patient was sitting on the bed complaining of smelling funny lasting for 10 seconds without any change in mental status or abnormal movements.  This episode was not associated with ictal change on EEG.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal video EEG monitoring.  No interictal epileptiform abnormalities were identified in this record.  The patient reported a smell of something funny, lasting 10 seconds.  At 1:21 p.m., on 08/08/2013, which were not associated with ictal change on EEG.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  This EEG does not rule out the diagnosis of epilepsy.  Please note that simple partial seizures are not always associated with changes on a scalp EEG.  The patient's habitual seizures were not captured.  If seizures are a clinical concern, a repeat prolonged video EEG monitoring may be helpful.

Hyperventilation is performed with fair effort and results in no significant change in the background.  Step photic stimulation does not result in any abnormalities.  A single EKG lead demonstrates a heart rate fewer than 60 beats a minute.
FINDINGS:
Excess beta.
No epileptiform discharges.
Heart rate less than 60 beats per minute.
IMPRESSION:  This is a normal awake and asleep 27-minute EEG.  The lack of epileptiform discharges in a routine EEG does not preclude a clinical diagnosis of epilepsy.  Excess beta could be secondary to medication effect such as benzodiazepines or barbiturates.  Heart rate fewer than 60 beats a minute is noted on the single EKG lead.

CLINICAL HISTORY:  An elderly man with confusion.
MEDICATIONS:  Zithromax, ceftriaxone, hydralazine, aspirin, lisinopril, others.
INTRODUCTION:  Digital video EEG was performed at the bedside using standard 10-20 system of electrode placement with 1 channel EKG.  The patient was agitated and uncooperative.  Electrode impedances were challenging.

The patient is not always responsive while awake and then demonstrates some inappropriate or involuntary behaviors while awake.

At the close of the record, the patient drifts off to sleep.  The background EEG demonstrates a more synchronously sleep slow pattern with a heart rate of 90 BPM.
IMPRESSION:  This is a technically challenging, but abnormal EEG due to:
Background disorganization and slowing.
Bursts of slow, rhythmic frontal delta.
CLINICAL CORRELATION:  The features described above are nonspecific.  They can be seen in the context of a toxic or metabolic encephalopathy, but chronic, age-related CNS process may also produce pattern.
